nature medicine
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-026-04494-4
Article
Clinical decision support in hematological 
malignancies using a case-grounded AI 
agent
In the format provided by the 
authors and unedited
Supplementary information
Supplementary Table 1 | Composition of the Clinical Case Memory (CCM): number of 
cases per disease entity. 
 
Supplementary Table 2 | De -identified case -level metadata across the CCM: newly 
diagnosed (n=907), relapsed/refractory (n=665), post CAR -T (n=344), and molecular 
tumorboard cases (n=107). 
 
Supplementary Table 3 | Similarity scores for the five highest -ranked cases retrieved by 
the Clinical Case Memory for a representative myeloma and leukemia query case, 
decomposed into entity match (0 -3), therapy phase (0 -2), therapy overlap (0 -3), and age 
proximity (0-2), with the composite similarity score per retrieved case. 
 
Supplementary Table 4 | Individual Likert -scale ratings (Q1 –Q6) for 45 complex 
hematological malignancy cases evaluated by four physicians across six LLM backbones 
in plain and HemaGuide -embedded (agent) mode. Q1 assessed tumor board 
concordance/clinical accuracy, Q2 guideline compliance/concordance, Q3 patient-specific 
contextualization and context integration, Q4 reasoning transparency and treatment 
sequencing, Q5 clinical utility and risk–benefit assessment, and Q6 run-to-run consistency 
and actionability. 
 
Supplementary Table 5 | Case -level evaluation data for the systematic ablation study 
across eleven configuration levels (L0 -L10), including subdimension scores and overall 
TBCS.                  
 
Supplementary Table 6 | Molecular Mode benchmark variant set: representative somatic 
variants with gold -standard ClinGen/CGC/VICC classifications (per Horak et al.) used 
for comparison to automated interpretation. 
 
Supplementary Table 7 | Case -level data for the simulated practice study, reporting 
treatment recommendations from four   resident physicians assessed unaided and with 
HemaGuide support alongside three senior subspecialist evaluations, with ground   truth, 
TBCS subdimension scores, and binary concordance judgments for each of the 30 cases. 
 
Supplementary Table 8 | Case -level concordance evaluation of HemaGuide on an 
independent multi-entity external validation cohort from LMU Munich, reporting ground 
truth, HemaGuide recommendation, TBCS with subdimension scores, and binary 
correctness adjudication.  
 
Supplementary Table 9 | Case -level evaluation of consecutive tumor board cases from a 
one-month prospective silent validation, reporting ground truth, HemaGuide 
recommendation, TBCS with subdimension scores and binary correctness assessment.  
 
Supplementary Table 10 | Token usage and processing time for HemaGuide across three 
operational stages: knowledge base extraction (2,041 CCM cases), structured case 
extraction (45 benchmark cases), and agent -based decision generation (45   benchmark 
cases), stratified by disease group (lymphomas, plasma cell dyscrasias, leukemias) and 
reporting duration (seconds), prompt tokens, completion tokens, and total tokens per 
case. 
 
Supplementary Table 11 | Classification of all discordant cases across the benchmark, 
silent trial, and external validation cohorts by entity and primary discordance reason 
(alternative regimen selection, overtreatment, undertreatment, workflow error, 
hallucination, contraindication non-recognition, or staging error).                       
 
Supplementary Table 12 | Lookup table mapping the abbreviated entity identifiers used 
throughout the study to their full clinical nomenclature.    
 
l_case_1
Age: 51
ECOG: 1
Primary diagnosis:
Acute myeloid leukemia, ID 01/2026
Classification according to WHO 2022: AML with CEBPA-Mutation
Classification according to ICC: AML with in-frame bZIP CEBPA-Mutation
At initial diagnosis:
Peripheral blood: Hb 9,4 g/dl, Plt 42/nl, WBC 62/nl, 88% blasts
Bone marrow aspiration and biopsy from 01.01.2026:
Cytology: Detection of 72% myeloid blasts
FACS: CD45 low' SSC low, CD34+' CD117+' HLA-DR+' CD38+, CD33+' CD13+, CD7-, CD56+/-,
CD64+
LAIP in the blast gate: 91 %
LAIP of CD45+ cells: 82 %
Karyotype (BM/PB): 46, XX [20] normal female karyotype
FISH (BM): Exclusion MECOM-, KMT2A- and CBFB-Rearrangement; no Detection of t(8;21),
t(9;22), t(15;17); no deletions 5q, 7q, 17p
Molecular genetics (BM): NPM1 neg., CEBPA pos. (biallelic: in-frame bZIP-Mutation +
N-terminal Frameshift-Mutation), FLT3-ITD neg., FLT3-TKD neg., IDH1/2 neg., BCR::ABL1
neg., DDX41 neg., TP53 WT, MLL-PTD neg.
Risk score (after ELN): Favorable risk
Secondary diagnoses:
None
Treatment and course:
Cytoreductive therapy with Hydroxyurea from 03.01.-12.01.26
Question for the tumor board:
Treatment initiation?
Tumor board decision:
Treatment initiation with Daunorubicin/Cytarabin (7+3 regimen). With Detection of a
In-Frame-Mutation in the bZIP-domain of CEBPA, additional administration of GO. In
case of response no indication for consolidating allogeneic stem cell transplantation.

l_case_2
Age: 48
ECOG: 2
Primary diagnosis:
Acute promyelocytic leukemia, AML M3 ID 01/26

Initial blood count: L 5,00/nl, Hb 5,0 g/dl, Plt 5/nl

Molecular genetics: PML::RARA pos.
FACS: CD45 low' SSC intermediate to high, CD34-' CD117+/- (low)' HLA-DR-' CD38+ (low),
CD33+' CD13+, CD64+ (99% in the blast gate, 85% the CD45+ cells)
Bone marrow aspiration:
Cytology: 70% promyelocytes, compatible with AML M3
FACS: CD45 low, SSC intermediate-high, CD34-, CD117+, HLA-DR-, CD38+, CD33+, CD13+,
CD64+ (95% in the blast gate, 95% the CD45+ cells)
Molecular genetics: PML::RARA pos.
Karyotype: 46, XX, t(15;17)(q24;q21) [7]
FISH: Detection of a translocation t(15;17)
Secondary diagnoses:
Allergies: none
Treatment and course:
ED AML M3 01.01.2026
Therapy ATO/ATRA and Idarubicin on 01.01.26 started.
Currently: Leukocytes 65.000/µl:
Question for the tumor board:
Treatment initiation
Tumor board decision:
Continuation of therapy with ATO/ATRA with high-risk APL under addition of HU 2g 2x
daily. No leukapheresis.

l_case_4
Age: 60
ECOG: 2
Primary diagnosis:
Acute lymphoblastic leukemia, common B-ALL type, ID 03/2024
At initial diagnosis:
Peripheral blood: Hb 8,2 g/dl, Plt 12/nl, Leukocytes 2,8/nl, 15% blasts
Bone marrow:
Cytology: 72% blasts
Histology: 90% blasts of a B-ALL. CD79a+, CD10+,
CD34+, TdT+, CD20(+) Ki-67 85%. Bcl6- CD23- CyclinD1- CD5- MPOFACS: CD45 low, SSC low,
CD34+, CD19+, CD10++
FACS: ED-LAIP (55 %): CD45 low, SSC low, CD34+, CD19+, CD10+, CD22+
Molecular genetics: BCR-ABL1 not detectable
Histology (reference pathology): 65 % infiltration B-lymphoblastic leukemia; MF-1
Secondary diagnoses:
Allergies: none
Treatment and course:
Currently:
Relapse of the common B-ALL
Bone marrow aspiration from 03.07.2025:
Cytology: 5% blasts
FACS: 22% blasts, Immunophenotype: CD34+, CD19+, CD10+, CD20-, CD22+, CD38+, TdT+,
CD33-, CD13-
Lumbar puncture from 08.07.2025:
Cytology: 1500 cells/µl, Detection of blasts
FACS: 90% blasts
Initiation of local therapy with intrathecal triple therapy 3x/week from the
10.07.2025
Initiation of systemic therapy with of a Dexamethason pre-phase therapy analogous the
GMALL recommendations
Newly developed motor impairments
Question for the tumor board:
Treatment recommendation?
Tumor board decision:
Relapse therapy with Inotuzumab ozogamicin; parallel intrathecal triple therapy for
treatment of the ZNS-Manifestation, after of a Cycle Inotuzumab systemic CNS-active
therapy (HD-MTX i.v. depending on general condition and blood count with or without
HD-Cytarabin); subsequently Continuation of therapy with Inotuzumab. CD19-CAR /
Allo-SCT to be considered.

l_case_5
Age: 72
ECOG: 1
Primary diagnosis:
Common B-ALL, ID 11/2021
At initial diagnosis:
Peripheral blood: Hb 7,2 g/dl, Plt 28/nl, WBC 3,5/nl, 12% blasts
Bone marrow:
Cytology: 88% blasts
Flow cytometry:
CD45 low, SSC low, CD34+, CD19+, CD20+/-, CD22+, CD10+, TdT+, CD33-, CD38+
FISH: unremarkable findings, no ALL-typical translocations
Histology: Interstitial and nodular infiltration, MF-0/1
Molecular genetics: BCR::ABL1 negative
Secondary diagnoses:
Breast carcinoma, right, ID 03/2023
- Stage: pT1c, pN0 (0/2 sn), L0, G2, R0, cM0
- Hormone receptor status: ER 80%, PR 60%, Ki67: 15%, HER2: negative
Treatment and course:
Question for the tumor board:
Treatment indication?
Tumor board decision:
With rising MRD positivity initially Treatment initiation with Blinatumomab. CAR-T /
Allo-SCT to be considered.
================================================================================

GMALL-elderly.
15.04.2020 CT H/T/A: presacral soft tissue formation without bone destruction (a.e.

glandular tissue left breast (if applicable, gynecological correlation). Small cystic
lesion pancreatic head ~0,5 cm (a.e. side branch dilatation).
18.04.2020 Lumbar puncture: 1 cell/µl; Flow cytometry without blast population.

(ED-LAIP not detectable).

a.e. extramedullary hematopoiesis.

22.08.2021 Start Inotuzumab-Ozogamicin (Cycle 3; administration on d1/d8/d15).

GMALL-elderly Pre-phase, subsequently Blinatumomab.

BCR::ABL1 negative

l_case_8
Age: 64
ECOG: 1
Primary diagnosis:
Acute lymphoblastic leukemia of the B-cell lineage (common B-ALL)
blood count before start of treatment: L 0,41/nl, Hb 9,1g/dl, T 33/nl, LDH 198U/l
BM cytology: 79% blasts
FACS: CD45-, SSC low, CD34 low, CD19+, CD20+, CD22+, Tdt+, CD10+, CD38+; 89% the CD45+
cells
Molecular genetics: BCR-ABL negative, IDH1 positive
Cytogenetics: 46,XY[1] normal male karyotype (findings with limited interpretive
value)
FISH: biallelic Deletion 9p21 (nullisomy), gain of chromosomal regions 9q22 and 9q34
as well as in the subclone on tumor cells with gain of chromosomal region 5q32q33
Secondary diagnoses:
History of appendectomy
Type 2 diabetes mellitus
Arterial hypertension
Treatment and course:
Molecular genetic findings
Question for the tumor board:
Pathogenic variant? Targeted Therapy?
Tumor board decision:
Targeted Therapy with Ivosidenib (+Azacitidin).

================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
==================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: If the patient is suitable for intensive care, a conventional induction 7+3
(cytarabine + daunorubicin) plus gemtuzumab ozogamicin (day 1/4/7) can be carried out without any
problems after a successful leukocyte reduction/prephase. For CR: consolidative HiDAC (1.5 g/m2 days
1-3, 3 cycles). For patients not suitable for intensive care: azacitidine + venetoclax or
participation in the study. Primary allo-SCT not indicated in CR1.
Rationale:
Rationale: In-frame bZIP-CEBPA corresponds favorably to ELN; Flowchart/guidelines recommend 7+3 + GO
and then HiDAC for younger patients requiring intensive treatment. Current hyperleukocytosis requires
a prephase (hydroxyurea, possibly zytarabine) before the start of induction. HMA+Ven as a valid
alternative if intensive fitness is lacking; allo-SCT only in case of recurrence/high-risk.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Continuation of cytoreduction (HU if necessary until WBC controlled) and, if the patient is capable
of intensive therapy, immediate intensive induction 7+3 (cytarabine + daunorubicin) with a single
administration of gemtuzumab ozogamicin in induction I. For CR: consolidative HiDAC 1.5 g/m2 days
1-3, a total of 3 cycles. HLA typing/donor search early; allo-SCT not primarily recommended.
HMA+Venetoclax only for non-fitness or study.
Rationale:
CEBPA bZIP in-frame corresponds to ELN favorable; Standard is intensive induction +/- GO
(guidelines/flowchart). High leukocytosis requires prephase (HU) until safe onset is possible.
According to CR, HiDAC (1.5 g/m2) is the consolidation standard. Allo only in
high-risk/refractoriness; HMA+Venetoclax for non-intensive candidates.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: For patients requiring intensive treatment, immediate intensive induction with 7+3
(cytarabine days 1-7 + daunorubicin days 3-5) after ensuring adequate leukocyte
control/leukoreduction phase. If age <60 and no contraindication, GO (Induction I). For CR,
consolidating HiDAC (1.5 g/m2 D1-3, 3 cycles). Primary allo-SCT not recommended in CR1. If not
suitable for intensive care: azacitidine+venetoclax or study participation.
Rationale:
CEBPA bZIP in-frame = ELN favorable; The best curative strategy for fit patients is intensive 7+3 +/-
GO followed by HiDAC. So far only hydroxyurea has been given for cytoreduction. Allo-SCT in CR1 not
indicated if risk is favorable. HMA+Venetoclax or study option if not suitable for intensive care.
================================================== gpt-5-mini (plain)
==================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Induction AML with CEBPA double mutations: 7+3 induction (daunorubicin 60-90 mg/m2 days 1-3;
cytarabine 100-200 mg/m2 days 1-7). After CR/CRi: HiDAC consolidation. Upfront allo-transplant only
in case of relapse or MRD-positive high-risk; If the risk is favorable, no upfront transplant.
Rationale:
CEBPA double mutations are considered a favorable AML risk; Standard induction + HiDAC consolidation
achieves good remission rates. Allo-transplantation in first remission is not mandatory if the risk
profile is good; Consider MRD-driven decisions in relapse.

===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Induction therapy AML 3+7: Daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 d1-3 + Cytarabine 100-200 mg/m2/d d1-7
(alternatively idarubicin 12 mg/m2 d1-3); after CR HiDAC consolidation. Allotransplantation only if
MRD positive or relapse.
Rationale:
CEBPA double mutation + FLT3 ITD neg -> favorable risk (ELN). No other high-risk factor. Target CR;
Transplantation usually not recommended in first remission; Transplant only if MRD positive/relapse.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Induction therapy AML: 7+3 induction (Daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 days 1-3 + Cytarabine 100-200 mg/m2 days
1-7). After CR MRD negativity, subsequent HiDAC consolidation; Allogeneic transplantation in first
remission only in cases of MRD positivity or clinical high-risk.
Rationale:
favorable ELN risk due to CEBPA biallelic mutations, normal karyotype, no FLT3-ITD; therefore
induction recommended, aiming for CR/MRD negativity; Establish HiDAC consolidation; SCT in first
remission only in MRD positivity or high-risk manifestations.
================================================== gpt-5-nano (agent)
==================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Induction: standard 7+3 (cytarabine + daunorubicin) in CEBPA double mutations/ELN-favored AML; If you
are fit (<60J), consider GO on days 1,4,7. Post-CR MRD-guided consolidation with HiDAC (1.5 g/m2,
days 1-3, 3 cycles). Allo-SCT in ER only in MRD persistence or high-risk; Check donors early.
Rationale:
CEBPA double mutations are ELN favored; MRD response determines further steps. 7+3 is established; GO
only if you are fit. Consolidation via HiDAC makes sense; allo-SCT in ER only risk-adapted in cases
of MRD persistence or high-risk; Check the donor pool early.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Induction: 7+3 (cytarabine 7d, daunorubicin 3d). GO possible at <60J. Consolidation controlled by MRD
with HiDAC 1.5 g/m2, 3 cycles at CR. Allo-SCT first remission: no standard with favorable ELN
(CEBPA); MRD positivity/relapse -> Check donor.
Rationale:
CEBPA-doublemut + favorable ELN -> good prognosis with chemo; Allo-SCT only after MRD
positivity/relapse; Donor availability taken into account.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Induction: 7+3 in CEBPA double mutant (bZIP-in-frame); GO optional if age appropriate. After CR/CRi
MRD-guided consolidation with HiDAC 1.5 g/m2 days 1-3, a total of 3 cycles. Allo-SCT in first
remission only with MRD-positive residual disease or lack of MRD negativity; screen donors early; if
ELN is favorable, no routine allo-SCT.
Rationale:
CEBPA double mutant AML with favorable ELN risk; high CR rate with standard induction. MRD status is
the key predictor of further steps. Routine allo-SCT in first remission is not recommended if the
risk is favorable; Residual MRD-positive or loss of MRD-negativity after consolidation increases need
for transplantation; GO option dependent on age/CD33.
================================================== gpt-5-nano (plain)
==================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:

Induction AML now: 3+7 standard (Daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 d1-3 + Cytarabine 100-200 mg/m2 d1-7). Target
remission. Then HiDAC consolidation (e.g. Cytarabine 3 g/m2 d1.3.5 x 3-4 cycles). SCT in CR1 only if
MRD positive/relapse; otherwise not with this favorable CEBPA mutation profile.
Rationale:
CEBPA double mutant AML belongs to favorable ELN risk; hence standard induction and MRD-guided
consolidation. Allogeneic transplantation in CR1 recommended only in cases of MRD-positive findings
or relapse; If the prognosis is otherwise good, no SCT in CR1. Hydroxyurea was cytoreduction, not a
contraindication.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:

(e.g. 3 g/m2 q12h on days 1,3,5). Allogeneic transplantation only in MRD-positive remission or
relapse.
Rationale:
CEBPA-mut AML (in-frame), ELN-favorable, no FLT3-ITD, normal KK; good remission rates with standard
induction. Hydroxyurea was cytoreduction. MRD-supported consolidation makes sense; Transplantation
not routinely in first remission with favorable risk.

===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Start hydroxyurea orally immediately: 4 g/day (2 tablets of 500 mg, 4x daily) for rapid
cytoreduction. ATRA and ATO continue. Leukapheresis contraindicated in APL. If there is no decline
after 48-72 hours or there is clinical leukostasis, consider short-term idarubicin according to the
APL regimen (e.g. days 1+3).
Rationale:
APL with current WBC 65,000/µl -> rapid cytoreduction necessary. Guidelines/flowchart: HU initial
measure (>50k: 4 g/d). Leukapheresis contraindicated. Anthracycline only in case of inadequate
response or life-threatening leukostasis. Only temporarily pause ATRA/ATO in the case of
differentiation syndrome; steroid prophylaxis/for DS dexamethasone 10 mg i.v. q12h. Bleeding
prophylaxis: platelet target >=30-50x10^9/L (for bleeding/intervention >=50), fibrinogen >=150 mg/dL,
aggressive substitution (FFP/KK/platelets). ECG/QTc monitoring under ATO.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
For PML::RARA+ APL with WBC 65,000/µl: Leukapheresis contraindicated. Immediate cytoreductive:
idarubicin immediately (e.g. idarubicin day 1+3) + continue ATRA unchanged; ATO according to the
protocol from day 5 (with QTc/EKG ok). If idarubicin is not available in the short term: temporary
hydroxyurea (e.g. 4 g/day) to anthracycline. Start with prophylactic steroid (e.g. prednisone 0.5-1
mg/kg p.o. or in case of high-risk/clinical signs dexamethasone 10 mg i.v. bid). Aggressive
coagulation optimization: fibrinogen >150 mg/dL, platelets >30-50,000/µL (transfusions, FFP/Cryo as
needed). Close clinical checks, daily blood counts, electrolytes, ECG (2x/week for ATO) and
monitoring for differentiation syndrome/TLS; If symptoms occur, pause ATRA/ATO and iv dexamethasone.
Rationale:
WBC >10,000 defines high-risk in APL -> Recommendation according to APL flowchart:
Anthracycline+ATRA; ATO only from day 5. Leukapheresis is contraindicated in APL. Hydroxyurea is
permitted as a temporary cytoreduction. Steroids prophylactically to prevent/mitigate the
differentiation syndrome; Aggressive coagulation prophylaxis reduces early bleeding and thrombosis
risks.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
IMMEDIATELY: Hydroxyurea 500 mg p.o. Start 4x/day (~4 g/day). No leukapheresis (APL
contraindication). Continue ATRA immediately; Also continue ATO with close QTc monitoring; Only
temporarily pause ATO/ATRA if differentiation syndrome symptoms are manifest. Steroid prophylaxis:
Prednisone 0.5 mg/kg/day (alternatively Dexa if clinically suspected). Bleeding prophylaxis:
thrombotransfusions so that Plt >30-50x10^9/L, fibrinogen >150 mg/dL (cryo/FFP as required). TLS
prophylaxis: rapid hydration, allopurinol; Rasburicase in hyperuricemia. Daily up to 2x daily
monitoring of Hb/Plt/WBC, coagulation, electrolytes, ECG. If there is no sufficient drop in WBC by
day 3 or if there are signs of leukostasis -> add short-term idarubicin (high-risk regimen, e.g. days
1+3).
Rationale:
Rationale: According to the APL flowchart, leukapheresis is contraindicated. For WBC >50k,
hydroxyurea is the first measure for cytoreduction; Idarubicin only in the absence of response or
clinical leukostasis. Steroids reduce risk of differentiation syndrome. ATRA/ATO should not be
routinely discontinued; Aggressively optimize coagulation parameters and transfusions.

Continuation of ATRA+ATO because the diagnosis of APL (t(15;17)) is present. Due to the high
leukocyte count (>=10,000/µl), an anthracycline induction (e.g. idarubicin 12mg/m2 i.v.) is added on
day 1-2. Prophylactic dexamethasone 10mg i.v. q12h for 7 days and if necessary hydroxyurea for rapid
leukocyte reduction.
Rationale:
APL with t(15;17) responds excellently to ATRA/ATO; With high-risk WBC values, an anthracycline
component is necessary to achieve remission and prevent differentiation syndrome. Dexamethasone
reduces the syndrome, hydroxyurea controls acute hyperleukocytosis.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Continuation of induction with ATRA+ATO. Immediate prophylactic dexamethasone therapy (10 mg IV q12h)
due to high leukocyte count (65,000/µl) and risk of differentiation syndrome. Additionally
hydroxyurea (50mg/kg/d) or low-dose cytarabine (100mg/m2 q12h) up to leukocytes <10,000/µl. Close
clinical and hematological control.
Rationale:
APL with t(15;17) reacts excellently to ATRA/ATO. The current leukocyte explosion risk requires
steroid prophylaxis and rapid leukocyte depletion to prevent differentiation syndrome.
Hydroxyurea/cytarabine is standard for WBC>40000/µl. No contraindications available.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Continuation of induction with ATRA+ATO. Immediate prophylactic dexamethasone therapy (10 mg IV q12h)
due to high leukocyte count and risk of differentiation syndrome. Rapid leukocyte reduction with
hydroxyurea (50mg/kg p.o. q6h) to <10000/µl. If hyperleukocytosis persists, additional idarubicin 12
mg/m2 on days 1, 3, 5.
Rationale:
APL with t(15;17) responds excellently to ATRA/ATO; intermediate risk does not require routine
chemotherapy, but leukocytosis increases the risk of differentiation syndrome. Steroid prophylaxis
and rapid cytoreduction prevent complications; Idarubicin is only used for persistent
hyperleukocytosis.
================================================== qwen3-32b (agent)
===================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:

Addition of hydroxyurea to current ATO/ATRA therapy for cytoreductive support in persistent
hyperleukocytosis.
Rationale:
For APL-M3 with PML::RARA translocation, the combination of ATO and ATRA is standard. According to
flowchart rule APL-3, persistent leukocytosis (>20,000/µl) requires additional measures with
hydroxyurea to prevent leukostasis and differentiation syndrome. ATO/ATRA are retained because they
enable molecular remission. QTc monitoring and coagulation management remain critical.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Continuation of ATO/ATRA with additive hydroxyurea cytoreduction for persistent leukocytosis. Closely
monitor for differentiation syndrome and QTc prolongation.
Rationale:
For APL-M3 with PML::RARA translocation, ATO/ATRA is the gold standard therapy. Persistent
leukocytosis (>20,000/µl) requires acute cytoreduction with hydroxyurea. Differentiation syndrome
(fever, shortness of breath, pleural effusion) must be excluded. QTc monitoring under ATO is
mandatory. No change in basic therapy as intermediate risk and no refractory proven.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Immediate cytoreduction with hydroxyurea (HU) according to the schedule (>50,000/µl: 2x500mg 4x/day)
and close monitoring for differentiation syndrome. Continue ATO/ATRA therapy, consider cytarabine
prephase if leukocytosis persists. Check QTc daily under ATO.
Rationale:
In APL-M3 with persistent hyperleukocytosis (65,000/µL), urgent cytoreduction is indicated. HU is
first choice, cytarabine prephase if response is inadequate. ATO/ATRA remains basic therapy,
differentiation syndrome prophylaxis with prednisone is already given. QTc monitoring is mandatory
for ATO.

================================================ qwen3-next-80b (agent)

===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Evaluate differentiation syndrome (DS) (fever, dyspnea, pulmonary infiltrates, hypotension). For DS:
pause ATO/ATRA, dexamethasone 10 mg iv twice daily, supportive. Without DS: Start hydroxyurea 500 mg
4x daily.
Rationale:
According to the APL flowchart: Passage leukocytosis >10,000/µl requires hydroxyurea, DS requires
ATO/ATRA break + dexamethasone. WBC 65,000/µl requires evaluation of DS symptoms. Without DS: HU
start according to WBC thresholds (50,000+ -> 4x500 mg).
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
1. Immediate evaluation for differentiation syndrome (dyspnea, fever, pulmonary infiltrates,
hypotension). 2. For DS: pause ATO/ATRA, dexamethasone 10 mg i.v. 2x daily >=3 days. 3. Without DS:
Hydroxyurea 2000 mg/day (4x500 mg) for WBC reduction. 4. Continuous WBC and clinical monitoring.
Leukapheresis contraindicated.
Rationale:
For APL with WBC >50,000/µl, hydroxyurea is indicated (Flowchart APL-3). Leukapheresis prohibited
(lead risk). DS clarification prioritized. For DS: dexamethasone and a break from therapy. Sanz
intermediate risk does not require chemotherapy add-on, only WBC control.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Evaluation for differentiation syndrome (dyspnea, hypoxia, pulmonary infiltrates, hypotension). If
present: pause ATO/ATRA, dexamethasone 10 mg i.v. 2x daily. Without DS symptoms: Start hydroxyurea
2000 mg/day (4x500 mg), continue ATO/ATRA.
Rationale:
According to APL flowchart (APL-3): Hydroxyurea is indicated for leukocytosis >50,000/µl without DS
symptoms. If DS symptoms occur, immediate ATO/ATRA break and dexamethasone. Prophylactic prednisone
already given (APL-1b), DS clarification prioritized.

===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:

therapy (age/comorbidity): azacitidine + venetoclax.
Rationale:
Therapy-associated AML/AML-MR with SF3B1 and unfavorable risk profile: CPX-351 is guideline
recommendation for AML-pCT/AML-MR and showed better results vs. 7+3. Due to adverse risk, allo-SCT is
indicated for consolidation. Lack of fitness -> HMA+Venetoclax as an alternative.
===== run_2 =====

(Vyxeos®) with immediate HLA typing/donor search and planned allogeneic SCT in CR. For non-intensive
fit: Azacitidine +/- Venetoclax.
Rationale:
Therapy-associated/AML-MR with SF3B1 and unfavorable risk -> guideline favorite CPX-351 for patients
requiring intensive treatment; allo-SCT is curatively indicated. Lack of fitness/high comorbidity:
HMA+/-Venetoclax. Clarify age, organ functions and CM histology and quickly start HLA/donor search.
===== run_3 =====

with CPX-351 (Vyxeos®) and initiate HLA typing/donor search at an early stage with planned allogeneic

venetoclax.
Rationale:
AML-pCT (SF3B1, MDS-associated) is considered unfavorable; Guidelines/flowchart recommend CPX-351 for
treatment-associated AML in patients requiring intensive treatment. Allo-SZT offers the best curative

and organ functions.

mutation). Age unknown, but AML-pCT commonly occurs in older patients. Breast cancer history (ED
2023) points to possible comorbidities. HMA + Venetoclax is standard in unfit or elderly patients at
unfavorable risk. Intensive chemotherapy (7+3) would be risky if age is not specified.
===== run_3 =====

Rationale:

The patient has a KMT2A rearrangement, KRAS mutations and failure of the venetoclax/azacitidine
combination. Current literature and similar cases show that intensive venetoclax-based combinations
(e.g. FLAG-IDA) achieve high remission rates and serve as a bridge to allogeneic SCT. Menin
inhibitors (Revumenib) are targeted for KMT2A-AML and can be added as part of a study.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
It is recommended that the patient be included in a menin inhibitor study arm (e.g. revumenib) or
compassionate use. If this is not possible, intensive venetoclax-combined salvage therapy (e.g.
FLAG-IDA+venetoclax) should be carried out as a bridge therapy up to allogeneic SCT.

Rationale:
KMT2A rearrangements respond strongly to menin inhibitors (Phase I/II data show high CR rates). After
failure of Ven/azacitidine, intensive venetoclax chemotherapy is still effective and allows remission
before allo-SCT. Similar cases use Venetoclax-Intensive-Salvage+allo-SCT.

===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
1) Immediate rapid NGS; if target mutation can be detected, primarily targeted therapy (e.g.
gilteritinib for FLT3, ivosidenib/enasidenib for IDH) +/- HMA. 2) If there is no target option:
non-intensive salvage with decitabine 10-day regimen (HMA monotherapy), venetoclax not preferred due
to previous treatment. 3) Consider DLI as immunological bridging in stages (in case of
cytoreduction/response), 4) 2. Allo-SCT only if CR has been achieved and the cardiac/general
condition is acceptable; Timing: as soon as possible after remission (4-8 weeks) as consolidation or
in the event of DLI failure.
Rationale:
Patient: AML-MRC with complex karyotype (-5/-7, der(17)), recurrence 24 M post-allo after
HMA+venetoclax -> high probability of venetoclax resistance; NYHA II makes intensive reinduction
risky. NGS can provide targeted, less toxic options. Mixed chimerism + mild cGVHD argue for DLI as a
useful immunological agent. Second transplant only if there is remission and sufficient organ
function; in the case of TP53/recurrent disease, prioritize alternative studies/palliative concepts.
===== run_2 =====

count, complex karyotype and NYHA II. Primary bridging to remission: decitabine 10 days (especially

(gilteritinib/ivosidenib/enasidenib). DLI only after hematological CR (if necessary combined with HMA
to increase chimeric switching). Second allo-SCT only as a curative option if CR has been achieved
and sufficient cardiac fitness.
Rationale:
Patient previously treated with HMA+venetoclax and allo-PBSCT, now relapse with unfavorable
cytogenetics (del(5q), -7, der(17)) and high tumor burden -> poor prognosis. NYHA II increases risk
for intensive anthracyclines/CPX-351. Decitabine 10-days has signal at TP53/complex karyotype;
Targeted inhibitors preferred if mutation is detected. DLI/2. Transplantation only after achieving a
CR and interdisciplinary risk assessment.

tumor burden; only after CR/MRD negativation as immune consolidation. 5) 2. allo-SCT only for CR and

Target: CR2; Allogeneic SCT is recommended for CR2. If refractoriness persists, consider
transplantation evaluation even without CR.
Rationale:
KIT p.D816V was classified as Likely Oncogenic (Horak et al. +8). Literature (PMID:40412578) shows
poorer prognosis of KIT mutations in CBF-AML and mentions KIT inhibitors (avapritinib, midostaurin)
as options to consider; Briquilimab not effective. Therefore intensive salvage +/- GO, KIT inhibitor
(study) and planning allo-SCT in CR2.
===== run_2 =====

Rationale:

genetics -> Guideline recommends observation for asymptomatic low-risk patients. The indication for

line. No cytogenetics, therefore no lenalidomide. Iron chelation in transfusion dependence. Regular
Hb monitoring.
===== run_2 =====

transfusion is required or Hb<10g/dl: use ESA (epoetin). Regular blood values, bone marrow check
every 6 months.
Rationale:
Low-risk MDS (IPSS-Rlow) without del(5q) or other risk factors. First-line therapy is observation or

===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:

Rationale:
Low-risk MDS with anemia, no karyotypic or molecular abnormalities. ESAs are the first choice for
transfusion-dependent anemia. Iron chelation prevents overload during chronic transfusion. No
aggressive therapies necessary.

================================================== qwen3-32b (agent)
===================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
The v. a. MDS diagnosis should be revised. A repeat bone marrow biopsy is recommended to confirm the
diagnosis and rule out other hypocellular syndromes. In confirmed MDS with transfusion-dependent
anemia, luspatercept should be considered first-line.
Rationale:
The lack of blasts/ring sideroblasts and unremarkable molecular profiles speak against classic MDS.
Hypocellular MDS are rare and require differential diagnoses such as aplastic anemias. Luspatercept
is evidence-based in transfusion-dependent LR-MDS. HMAs are not standardized in low-risk MDS.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
The v. a. MDS diagnosis should be revised. A comprehensive diagnosis according to the flowchart (DX-1
to DX-5) is recommended to rule out differential diagnoses (e.g. aplastic anemia, reactive changes).
If hypoplastic MDS is confirmed (WHO 2022), immunosuppression (ATG + cyclosporine) should be
considered. If uncertain: repeat the bone marrow biopsy with a focus on cellularity.
Rationale:
The diagnosis of MDS risk group low (IPSS-M/R) with normal molecular and cytogenetic diagnostics
suggests hypoplastic MDS (new WHO entity). Absence of blasts/ring sideroblasts and normal karyotype
support this. Differential diagnoses such as aplastic anemia or reactive changes must be excluded.
Treatment options if confirmed: immunosuppression or watch & wait if uncertain.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
The v. a. MDS diagnosis should be revised taking into account the hypocellular bone marrow findings
and absence of molecular/cytogenetic aberrations. Differential diagnoses such as aplastic anemia or
reactive changes must be excluded. Comprehensive diagnostics according to the flowchart (DX-1 to
DX-5) including telomere length analysis (in young patients) and exclusion of Fanconi anemia are
recommended. If a diagnosis of MDS (hypoplastic) is confirmed, immunosuppression (ATG + cyclosporine
A) should be considered.
Rationale:
The diagnosis of MDS risk group low with missing blasts/ring sideroblasts and normal molecular
findings suggests hypoplastic MDS (WHO 2022). However, differential aplastic anemia and reactive
changes must be excluded. The lack of clonality in molecular testing supports revision of the
diagnosis. If hypoplastic MDS is confirmed, immunosuppression is indicated, otherwise further
treatment towards aplastic anemia is indicated.

Rationale:
Accelerated CML (13% blasts) with progression on bosutinib -> dasatinib (failure to two 2GTKIs).

Rationale:
CML accelerating phase with 13% blasts and ELTS score 2.935 (poor prognosis). Two TKI lines
(bosutinib, dasatinib) failed. Ponatinib shows good efficacy in recurrent/progressive cases and
overcomes common resistance mechanisms.

maintenance with ponatinib, plus CNS prophylaxis and close MRD monitoring. Vascular risk (DM)
requires dose reduction/monitoring. In case of MRD reactivation, use secondary salvage options
(blinatumomab +/- TKI, CAR-T).
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: Since MRD-negative CR was achieved under ponatinib and AlloTx was rejected,
continuation of the GMALL-EVOLVE protocol: complete all planned consolidation cycles including
complete CNS prophylaxis; followed by maintenance phase with ponatinib (long-term) according to
protocol. Close qPCR MRD controls (initially monthly). Cardiometabolic monitoring.
Rationale:
Rationale: Ph+ ALL showed MRD-neg CR under ponatinib; for patients without AlloTx, the best strategy
is consistent TKI-based consolidation and maintenance. Maintain ponatinib due to proven efficacy. In
case of MRD recurrence, consider blinatumomab or a change in therapy. Control CV risks/diabetes and
adjust TKI dose according to risk.

algorithm. Allo-SCT is the only curative option if a CR is achieved. The tumor is CD7 positive, so a
CD7 CAR T trial is a sensible, possibly superior option.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Start of nelarabine-based salvage therapy (e.g. nelarabine+dexamethasone+/-cyclophosphamide) with
target CR, followed by allogeneic SCT in remission; If a suitable course of study is available (e.g.
CD7-CAR-T), simultaneous examination and, if necessary, admission.
Rationale:
T-LBL after GMALL induction and consolidation is progressive. According to GMALL and ALL guidelines,
nelarabine is the preferred salvage regimen for T-ALL/LBL, followed by allo-SCT as the curative

option. One study arm (CD7-CAR-T) can further increase the remission rate.

================================================== qwen3-32b (agent)
===================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: CD7-targeted CAR T cells (BE-CAR7) as the next therapeutic step. Alternative options:
daratumumab or bortezomib.
Rationale:
According to evidence, BE-CAR7 (CD7-specific) is the priority option for recurrent TLBL after
GMALL/KonsIV therapy with PD. CAR-T cells show maximum effectiveness in similar cases.
Daratumumab/bortezomib are secondary alternatives.
===== run_2 =====

Rationale:
For TLBL with PD after GMALL and KonsIV, nelarabine or cyclophosphamide are indicated as salvage
options according to guidelines (Flowchart V.3). PET-adapted therapy and CNS protection are crucial.
Allo-SCT remains a long-term option in remission.
===== run_3 =====

======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Prompt orchiectomy (if not already done) and complete staging including MRI BWK10 and brain MRI +
cerebrospinal fluid. Then rapid start of therapy: 6x R-CHOP + 2x systemic HD-MTX as CNS prophylaxis
(systemic preferred over only i.th.). Contralateral testicular irradiation recommended after chemo.
Unclear BWK10 before therapy via MRI/if necessary. Clarify biopsy.
Rationale:
Consistent with guidelines: primary testis DLBCL requires orchiectomy, R-CHOP and HD-MTX due to
increased CNS risk. Systemic HD-MTX has a better cerebral effect than just i.th. Contralateral RT
reduces local recurrence. BWK10 influences staging/CNS-IPI -> clarification necessary.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
If not already done: immediate radical orchiectomy on the right. Then 6x R-CHOP21 with CNS
prophylaxis by 2x systemic HD-MTX (intercalating or sequential). After systemic therapy, irradiation
of the contralateral testicle (alternative: bilateral orchiectomy). Clarify BWK-10 using MRI before
final staging; Biopsy only if malignancy is highly suspected.
Rationale:
Primary DLBCL of the testis has high CNS risk; Guidelines/SOP: Orchiectomy + 6x R-CHOP and systemic
HD-MTX are considered standard. Intrathecal prophylaxis alone is usually not enough. Contralateral
scrotal irradiation reduces local recurrences. BWK findings can influence staging and should be
clarified using MRI (biopsy if necessary).
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: If not already done, immediate radical right orchiectomy. Subsequently, systemic
first-line therapy 6x R-CHOP21. CNS prophylaxis: 2 cycles of systemic HD-MTX (systemically preferred
before/in addition to i.th.; i.th. alone not sufficient). Irradiation of the contralateral
testicle/scrotum recommended. BWK 10 lesions should be quickly clarified with MRI; Only consider
biopsy if MRI findings are unclear. Carry out staging/basic diagnostics (ECG/echo, laboratory,
HBV/HCV/HIV) before starting therapy, do not delay the initiation of therapy unnecessarily.
Rationale:
Primary DLBCL of the testis has increased CNS risk; Guidelines/flowchart recommend orchiectomy + 6x
R-CHOP and consideration of 2x HD-MTX as CNS prophylaxis. Contralateral testicular irradiation
reduces local recurrence. BWK-10 can change staging/CNS-IPI -> MRI for differentiation (biopsy only
if bone involvement is suspected).

benign hemangioma - no therapy, only radiological control.
Rationale:
Testicular lymphomas have a high CNS risk; Flowchart A4 recommends R-CHOP + HD-MTX prophylaxis and
testis radiation. Intrathecal MTX is not necessary when systemic HD-MTX is given. The thoracic lesion
corresponds to a benign hemangioma, therefore no oncological intervention.
======================================================== gpt-oss-120b (plain)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Start systemic therapy with R-CHOP (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine,
prednisone) for 6 cycles combined with CNS prophylaxis (intrathecal or high-dose methotrexate). After
completion of chemotherapy, local involvement field radiotherapy (~30Gy) is carried out on the
affected testicle.
Rationale:
Primary testicular DLBCL is a high-risk lymphoma with a high rate of CNS infiltration. R-CHOP is
standard chemo, rituximab improves response. CNS prophylaxis reduces the risk of brain and spinal
cord metastases. Scrotal radiotherapy improves local control.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Start systemic therapy with 6 cycles of R-CHOP (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin,
vincristine, prednisone) combined with cerebral prophylaxis (intrathecal methotrexate +/- high-dose
systemic MTX). After completion of chemotherapy, involved-field radiotherapy (30Gy) is carried out on
the affected testicle and, if necessary, prophylactically on the contralateral testicle.
Rationale:
Primary DLBCL of the testis is a highly aggressive non-Hodgkin lymphoma with a high risk of cerebral
metastases. R-CHOP is standard first-line regime; additional CNS prophylaxis reduces the risk of
leptomeningeal infiltration. Local radiotherapy improves local control after chemotherapy.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Start systemic therapy with R-CHOP (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine,
prednisone) for 6-8 cycles, combined with CNS prophylaxis (intrathecal or high-dose methotrexate).
After completion of chemotherapy, local testicular radiotherapy (30Gy) should be given to reduce the
risk of recurrence.
Rationale:
Primary DLBCL of the testis is an aggressive, extranodal lymphoma with a high risk of CNS and
testicular recurrences. R-CHOP is standard first-line therapy; CNS prophylaxis reduces the occurrence
of brain/spinal cord involvement. Local radiotherapy after chemo improves the local control rate.

===== run_1 =====

===== run_2 =====

Rationale:

===== run_3 =====

Orchiectomy is diagnostically and therapeutically relevant. PET/CT shows focal lymph node
infiltration, which is considered in testicular DLBCL therapy.

======================================================== qwen3-next-80b (agent)

===== run_1 =====

======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Curative-intended first-line therapy is recommended. In fit patients: 6x Pola-R-CHP (preference for
IPI 2-5); if not available/contraindicated: 6x R-CHOP21. No routine CNS prophylaxis.
Rationale:
IPI 3, LVEF 58% allowed anthracycline; SOP/Flowchart recommends 2-5 Pola-R-CHP for IPI. advance.
CNS-IPI ~3 (renal involvement, LDH↑, stage IV) -> no indication for intrathecal or HD-MTX
prophylaxis. CKD 3a increases the risk of HD-MTX, therefore additionally contraindicated without a
clear CNS risk factor.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: Start curatively intended systemic therapy. For fit patients: 6x Polatuzumab-R-CHP
(preferred option); alternatively 6x R-CHOP. No routine CNS prophylaxis (CNS IPI prerequisite 3). MRI
skull + CSF only if symptoms or CNS-IPI >=4. Pay attention to cardiac monitoring and renal
adjustment.
Rationale:
DLBCL NOS, IPI 3 (stage IV, LDH↑, kidney involvement). Guidelines/Flowchart: Pola-R-CHP preferred for
IPI 2-5; LVEF 58% allows anthracycline. CNS prophylaxis is recommended for CNS IPI >=4 or
testis/mammary/epidural. CKD3a increases risk of HD-MTX; Intrathecal administration has limited
preventive effect for parenchymal CNS disease.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Start curatively intended systemic therapy: 6x polatuzumab-R-CHP (if not available: 6x R-CHOP).
Anthracycline permitted (LVEF 58%). No routine CNS prophylaxis (neither i.th. nor HD-MTX) if
estimated CNS-IPI ~3. For neurology or CNS-IPI >=4: MRI skull + CSF. Monitor kidney function closely
and make dosage adjustments.
Rationale:
DLBCL NOS with IPI 3 -> intensive first-line therapy according to SOP; Pola-R-CHP preferred at IPI
2-5. CNS risk (kidney, LDH, stage) results in CNS-IPI ~3, therefore no indication for prophylactic
HD-MTX/IT-MTX; HD-MTX is also contraindicated due to CKD 3a. EOT-PET/CT and review in lymphoma
conference.
========================================================= gpt-5-mini (plain)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Initiation of therapy: R-CHOP-21 (rituximab + cyclophosphamide + doxorubicin + vincristine +
prednisone) 6 cycles, primary G-CSF prophylaxis. Because of kidney disease: CNS prophylaxis -- if
kidney function is sufficient (GFR >=50-60), intercalating high-dose IV methotrexate; otherwise
intrathecal methotrexate. Nephro and TLS monitoring.
Rationale:
DLBCL, CD20+, Stage IV, IPI 3 - Standard firstline is R-CHOP; LVEF 58% allows anthracycline. CKD 3a
allows R-CHOP without general dose reduction, requires monitoring. Kidney involvement increases the
risk of CNS recurrence -> targeted CNS prophylaxis. G-CSF due to risk/treatment intensity.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:

Initiation of R-CHOP q21d (rituximab + CHOP) as first-line, planned 6 cycles (response dependent).
Before starting: HBV serology, TLS prophylaxis (hydration, allopurinol/possibly rasburicase),
baseline laboratory including creatinine, ECG/echo. Check CNS risk via CNS-IPI; If the risk is high,
consider IT prophylaxis or systemic HD-MTX after renal function assessment.
Rationale:
Treatment-naïve CD20+ DLBCL St.IV with IPI3 - R-CHOP is standard firstline and possible with LVEF
58%. Kidney involvement increases tumor lysis and CNS risk, therefore TLS prophylaxis, close
monitoring and HBV check before rituximab. CKD3a requires caution/dose review with HD methotrexate.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Initiation of therapy: R-CHOP-21 (6 cycles in total) with primary G-CSF prophylaxis. Check HBV
serology and vaccination status before rituximab. Intermediate PET after 2 cycles. Because of kidney
involvement and IPI 3: CNS prophylaxis - if the kidney function is sufficient, systemic HD
methotrexate (e.g. 3 g/m2 i.v. between cycles 2/3 and 5/6), otherwise intrathecal prophylaxis.
Nephrological monitoring and dose adjustment if necessary.
Rationale:
New, untreated CD20+ DLBCL Stage IV - R-CHOP is standard. CKD 3a usually allows R-CHOP but requires
monitoring/dose adjustment. Kidney involvement and increased IPI increase CNS risk, therefore
consider prophylaxis. G-CSF reduces febrile neutropenia; HBV screening necessary because of
rituximab; Intermediate PET for therapy control.
======================================================== gpt-5-nano (agent)
========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: First-line therapy with R-CHOP-6 (R-CHOP-21) for advanced DLBCL NOS with
spleen/kidney involvement, LDH↑, IPI 3. DA-EPOCH-R is not preferable due to CKD3a/side effects and no
double hit. CNS prophylaxis only examined in study; Note PET-controlled consolidation.
Rationale:
R-CHOP-6 is established and better tolerated in CKD 3a; MYC-IHC pos without translocation has no
clear advantage for DA-EPOCH-R; CNS prophylaxis not standard outside studies; Spleen/kidney
involvement increases CNS risk, prophylaxis only makes sense in the studio context.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
First-line for advanced DLBCL NOS (St IV, IPI 3, MYC-IHC+): 6 cycles of Pola-R-CHP (Pola replaces
vincristine). Pay attention to CKD 3a, adjust dose if necessary. CNS prophylaxis per CNS-IPI
(consider if >=4 intrathecal MTX). Interim PET after 2 cycles to determine duration of therapy.
include lymphoma conference; review clinical trials.
Rationale:
MYC-IHC+ without DH; DA-EPOCH-R has no clear advantage in unselected DLBCL; Pola-R-CHP shows
advantages in the POLARIX trial; CKD 3a increases Tox; Spleen/kidney involvement increases risk; CNS
prophylaxis makes sense if the CNS IPI is high; close monitoring via interim PET.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
DA-R-EPOCH-R as first-line therapy for advanced DLBCL NOS (stage IV, spleen and kidney involvement,
LDH↑, IPI 3, MYC-IHC+) with CNS prophylaxis (intrathecal MTX; CKD3a-adapted dose). 6 cycles, interim
and final PET for residual storage; Radiotherapy only for residual bulk after EOT-PET.
Rationale:
High-risk phenotype (IPI 3, spleen/kidney involvement, LDH↑, MYC-IHC+) -> DA-R-EPOCH-R enables better
remissions than R-CHOP with suitable fitness. CNS risk high -> prophylaxis makes sense. CKD3a limits
high-dose MTX; prefers IT prophylaxis. Close lymphoma conference for progression.
======================================================== gpt-5-nano (plain)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====

Tumor board decision:
CAR T-cell therapy (CD19) as the next therapeutic step for relapsed/refractory DLBCL after previous
treatment; Bridge therapy with BR or GemOx if waiting; Check CAR-T availability.
Rationale:
MYC-positive, highly aggressive DLBCL (Ki-67 75-85%), IPI 3; no double/triple hit. CAR-T offers
highest remission rate in R/R DLBCL. LVEF 58% and CKD IIIa; no contraindications. Bridge makes sense
to achieve disease control through CAR-T.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
CAR-T cell therapy (axicabtagene ciloleucel or tisagenleucel) as the next therapeutic step for
refractory/stage IV DLBCL after Frontline. suitability assessment; Bridging therapy (e.g. reduced BR
or R-ICE) for remission/ASCT bridge, then CAR-T.
Rationale:
Very high risk (IPI 3, B symptoms, multisystem involvement including spleen, MYC+; Ki-67 75-85%),
progression to frontline. CAR-T offers the best chance of long-term remission. Autologous
transplantation as an alternative, but CAR-T preferred; CKD 3a requires dose-adjusted bridging
therapy.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
CAR T-cell therapy (axicabtagene ciloleucel or lisocabtagene maraleucel) as second-line in
refractory/relapsed DLBCL; Bridging if necessary If CAR-T is not available or contraindicated:
Polatuzumab vedotin + bendamustine + rituximab (Pola-BR) as bridging/salvage with ASCT option.
Rationale:
High risk biology (MYC+, Ki-67 75-85%), earlier progression after first line. CAR-T offers best
remission rates and cure potential in R/R DLBCL. Splenic extranodal findings do not influence
prognosis against CAR-T; CKD 3a limits nephrotoxic options; quick treatment needed. If CAR-T is not
possible promptly: Pola-BR/Salvage+ASCT.

======================================================== gpt-oss-20b (plain)

===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Start R-CHOP-14 (R-CHOP with 14-day interval) with renal-adapted cyclophosphamide (~500mg/m2) and
G-CSF support. Rituximab + CHOP standard for DLBCL IV, IPI3, MYC positive, without double hit.
Regular renal evaluation and LDH monitoring.
Rationale:

===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Start R-CHOP
(rituximab+cyclophosphamide750mg/m2+doxorubicin25mg/m2+vincristine1.4mg/m2+prednisolone100mg/m2)
21-day q cycle, 6 cycles, with renal-adapted cyclophosphamide and doxorubicin. Monitor LVEF and renal
function regularly. If the response is inadequate after 3 cycles: R-CHOP-14 or R-CHOEP (with
dose-reduced etoposide).
Rationale:
DLBCLIV, IPI3, MYC positive, no double hit FISH. Standard R-CHOP is the first choice. CKD3a requires
cyclophosphamide and doxorubicin dose reduction (750mg/m2 and 25mg/m2, respectively) to avoid renal
and cardiac toxicity. 6 cycles at 21 day intervals are evidence-based. If response is poor, more
intensive R-CHOP-14 or R-CHOEP may be considered, with dose reduction of etoposide to minimize renal
burden.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Start R-CHOP (Rituximab 375mg/m2 + Cyclophosphamide 75% of the standard dose, Doxorubicin 50mg/m2,
Vincristine 1.4mg/m2, Prednisolone 100mg/m2) every 14 days for 6 cycles, with constant monitoring of
kidney function and further dose adjustment if necessary.
Rationale:
Standard therapy for DLBCLIV without double hit gene; for CKD3a, cyclophosphamide dose must be
reduced by 25%; R-CHOP-14 offers optimal effectiveness and tolerable renal stress in aggressive
disease.

close monitoring of renal function
Rationale:
Standard first line for DLBCL NOS without double/triple hit. CKD 3a allows R-CHOP at standard dose
(no renal excretion of vincristine/cyclophosphamide). LVEF 58% and CD20+ speak for rituximab. Renal
involvement requires monitoring but no dosage adjustment is necessary.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Initiate R-CHOP therapy (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisolone) with
adjusted cyclophosphamide dose (750 mg/m2) and strict renal function monitoring.
Rationale:
Standard first-line therapy for DLBCL NOS without double/triple hit. CKD 3a allows R-CHOP with dose
adjustment and monitoring. Kidney disease and LDH elevation are taken into account in IPI, no
contraindications for R-CHOP (LVEF 58%, no allergies).
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Initiate R-CHOP therapy with close renal function monitoring and tumor lysis syndrome prophylaxis
Rationale:
DLBCL stage IV with IPI 3 requires standard therapy R-CHOP. No double/triple hit, therefore no
intensive therapy. CKD 3a allows R-CHOP without dose adjustment, but monitoring is required.
Cyclophosphamide metabolism hepatic, no significant renal excretion.

#### CASE: 4
###############################
======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Continuation of epcoritamab as bridging. Restaging (PET/CT) after 2 additional cycles; in case of
metabolic CR or clear PR: plan allo-SCT as consolidation. The 8-cm pulm persists. Lesion
metabolically active or only slightly regressive: primary surgical resection for technical
feasibility and sufficient lung reserve; otherwise thoracic radiotherapy (ISRT/SBRT, e.g. ~30-36 Gy).
Allo-SCT ideally 4-8 weeks after local therapy or as soon as the best possible response has been
achieved. If system progression occurs under epcoritamab, consider alternative systemic therapy/study
and accelerate rapid transplant planning.
Rationale:
After CAR-T failure, BiTE (epcoritamab) is a guideline-consistent option; The aim is to minimize
residual disease before allo-SCT. An isolated bulky thoracic lesion impairs transplant
prognosis-local consolidation (OP preferred, otherwise RT) improves chance of NED. Time window 4-8
weeks after local therapy allows recovery before conditioning.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Continue epcoritamab as bridging; Re-staging (PET-CT) after another 2-3 cycles. If metabolic CR/PR
(Deauville <=3), plan allo-SCT. If there is a persistent metabolically active bulky lesion (>5-6 cm)
or no response: primary surgical resection if feasible and tolerated by the patient, otherwise
thoracic ISRT (~30-36 Gy) before rapid allotherapy (4-6 weeks after best response).
Rationale:
BiTE (epcoritamab) is suitable systemic therapy after CAR-T failure; The goal is low tumor burden
before Allo. Bulky, metabolically active residual tumors increase transplant risk -> local control
(OP or ISRT) if systemic response is inadequate. PET-driven decision and rapid timing recommended.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Continue epcoritamab primarily as systemic bridging. Restaging (PET-CT) after 2-3 cycles. For CR or
clear PR: rapid allo-SCT. For persistent PET-positive/bulky pulmonary residual (especially >5 cm or
satellite): locoregional consolidation before Allo (preferred ISRT 36-40 Gy; resection only if
complete R0 is technically favorable). If there is no response: switch to alternative BiTE/study or
palliative local therapy.
Rationale:
After CAR-T failure, BiTEs such as epcoritamab are suitable systemic bridging options; The aim is to
achieve the best possible systemic remission before allo-SCT. A large, isolated, PET-active focus
increases graft failure, therefore local consolidation (RT or surgery) is recommended, allo as soon
as best possible response is achieved (PET-neg ideal, otherwise major PR).

approach with possible ASCT is standard; CAR-T is reserved for patients who are transplant ineligible
or after ASCT failure.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Indication for CAR T-cell therapy (axi-cel/tisa-cel/liso-cel) as second line for early relapse (DS5,
<3 months after EOT) of the activated B-cell type of DLBCL. Before biopsy confirmation, bridging
therapy if necessary, then leukapheresis and transfer to a certified CAR-T center.
Rationale:
Patient has only one previous chemo-immunotherapy (R-polatuzumab-CHP) and shows early metabolic
progression (DS5). According to current guidelines, CAR-T is approved for refractory/relapsed DLBCL
after >=1 prior therapy. Comorbidities (AHT, type II DM) do not represent a contraindication.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:

recurrence (ABC) is found, immediately initiate CAR T cell therapy (axi-cel/tisa-cel/liso-cel), if
necessary with bridging therapy, since early metabolic PD after R-Pola-CHP and lack of transplant
suitability.
Rationale:

setting, and the patient has no absolute contraindications.

======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Yes - histological confirmation is recommended before initiating systemic salvage
therapy/allo-SCT/study inclusion. Primary: CT-guided transthoracic core biopsy of a peripheral
FDG-active nodule; if central: bronchoscopic biopsy including BAL. Pathology: H&E, IHC (CD19, CD20,
CD79a, Ki-67), clonality, FISH; parallel microbiological/PNP clarifications.
Rationale:
Re-biopsy is SOP/flowchart standard in case of progression/recurrence (also post-CAR-T). Goal:
Clarify tumor vs. inflammation/infection, check CD19/CD20 status for further CD19-targeted
therapies/BiTE/allo-SCT and confirm any histological changes/therapy decision. If a biopsy is
impossible: comprehensive infectious disease clarification and interdisciplinary decision-making.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Yes. Before starting systemic salvage therapy, allo-SCT or study inclusion, the pulmonary FDG-active
nodules should be histologically confirmed (primarily CT-guided percutaneous core biopsy or
bronchoscopically). Additional: IHC for CD19/CD20, FISH, NGS and microbiological examinations.
Rationale:
After progression after CD19-CAR-T, clarification: malignant vs inflammation/infection and recording
of the CD19/CD20 status are mandatory, as CD19 loss influences therapy options (BiTEs, renewed
CD19-targeted approaches, allo-SCT) and study screening. Lung biopsy is usually safe and crucial to
treatment.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Before initiating systemic salvage therapy, allo-SCT or study inclusion: histological confirmation of
the new pulmonary FDG-active nodules (CT-guided punch biopsy or bronchoscopic biopsy). Request
pathology including immunohistochemistry and flow cell analyzes (at least CD19, CD20, Ki-67).
Rationale:
After CAR-T, there is risk of antigen loss (CD19) and DD from inflammation/infection. Re-biopsy is
SOP/guideline compliant, clarifies tumor progression and provides crucial target antigen information
for choosing BiTE vs anti-CD19 strategies or allo-SCT/studies.

indications; Critically assess polatuzumab re-exposure.
Rationale:

Bispecific antibodies are currently considered the preferred option after CAR-T failure; anti-CD19
ADC or second CAR-T are possible subsequently. Allo-SCT remains a consolidation option if remission
is achieved and good fitness is achieved. Checkpoint inhibitors have limited evidence.
===== run_2 =====

stressful alternative.

#### CASE: 8
###############################
======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Before deciding on therapy: another tissue biopsy to rule out histological transformation. If
transformation is missing: targeted therapy with tazemetostat (EZH2 inhibitor) 800 mg p.o. twice
daily (evidence: phase II; approval/studies for EZH2-mut. R/R FL). Parallel testing of bispecific T
cell engagers/clin. Studies and Allo-SCT if suitable.
Rationale:
EZH2 p.Y646H is classified as oncogenic (+10; Horak et al. 2022). Phase II data (PMID:34159682) show
high response rate in EZH2 mutant R/R FL; Matching analyzes (PMID:35157216) suggest a more favorable
safety profile compared to PI3K inhibitors. Early progression after CAR-T justifies biopsy to rule
out transformation before conversion.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Before deciding on therapy: another tissue biopsy to rule out histological transformation. Without
transformation: targeted therapy with tazemetostat 800 mg BID (EZH2 mutated; phase II/regulatory
evidence). Alternatively, study inclusion (bispecific T cell engagers) or allo-SCT if suitable.
Rationale:
EZH2 p.Y646H is classified as oncogenic (Horak +10; hotspot, functionally confirmed). Phase II data
show high response rate in R/R FL (ORR ~76%, PMID:34159682); Matching analyzes suggest better safety
record vs PI3K inhibitors (PMID:35157216). Biopsy recommended due to early progression after CAR-T.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Before definitive therapy: repeat tissue biopsy to exclude histological transformation. In the
absence of transformation and confirmed EZH2 p.Y646H mutation, preferred therapy: Tazemetostat 800 mg
p.o. twice daily (Phase II evidence). Parallel: Recommend inclusion in a study with bispecific T cell
engagers; If suitable, consider Allo-SCT.
Rationale:
EZH2 p.Y646H is classified as an oncogenic (Horak et al. +10). Phase II study in EZH2-mutated R/R FL
showed ORR ~76.5% with tazemetostat (PMID:34159682). Indirect comparison shows more favorable safety
profile vs PI3K inhibitors (PMID:35157216). Rebiopsy due to early progression after CAR-T to clarify
therapy planning.

=========================================================
===== run_1 =====

response and with good performance status.

bendamustine + rituximab (pola-BR) after unsuccessful CAR-T therapy. This regimen achieved an ORR
>60% in phase 2 trials (N=120) and is approved for R/R DLBCL without an ASCT option.
Rationale:
Variant is clearly oncogenic and is directly targeted by polatuzumab (anti-CD79B). Phase-1b/2 (2024)
and Phase-2 (2021) show high ORR/CRR. CD79B mutation signals worse prognosis, hence intensive
approach. No relevant FISH change found.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Polatuzumab-vedotin 1.8mg/kg + bendamustine 90mg/m^2 + rituximab 375mg/m^2 q3w, 6cycles (PhaseII
du2021) - targeted ADC against CD79B mutated. Alternatively, mosunetuzumab + polatuzumab (Phase1b/2
2024) remains options.

Rationale:
Variant CD79Bp.Y197C is oncogenic (+10 points). No FISH-abnormalities. Literature: 2024
Mosunetuzumab+Pola (phase1b/2) and 2021 Pola+BR (phaseII) show ORR/CRR >30% in CD79B-positive
R/R-DLBCL. Consequently, the recommended Pola combination is evidence-based.

mosunetuzumab+polatuzumab (Phase1b/2) and polatuzumab+bendamustine+rituximab (Phase2). The mutation
is prognostically unfavorable, so targeted CD79b therapy is recommended after CAR-T failure.

======================================================== qwen3-next-80b (agent)

===== run_1 =====

CD79B p.Y197C is oncogenic (Horak 2022). Polatuzumab vedotin (anti-CD79B ADC) shows efficacy in R/R
DLBCL with CD79B mutations (PMID:33942442, CRR 43%). pola-BR is established as a standard therapy.
Mosunetuzumab combination shows promise in trials (PMID:38072960).

======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
BCL2 p.A131V (Likely Oncogenic). Recommendation: venetoclax-based therapy preferably as part of a
clinical study (e.g. ViPOR) or as a rational off-label combination. Alternative/complementary
options: Loncastux, tafasitamab+lenalidomide; If there is a chemosensitive response, check Allo-SCT.
Consider participating in alternative CAR-T studies. Mandatory before making a decision: re-biopsy
(IHC CD19/CD20, EBER), CAR-T persistence qPCR, NGS and PET staging.
Rationale:
BCL2 p.A131V was classified as Likely Oncogenic (hotspot A131V, absent in gnomAD, OncoKB support).
PubMed (PMID:38899693) describes venetoclax in ViPOR (phase1b-2) in R/R DLBCL, rationale for BCL2
inhibition. Approved options (Loncastux, Tafasitamab+Len) and studies for renewed CAR-T or Allo-SCT
are relevant alternatives. Additional diagnostics are crucial for therapy.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
No directly targeted therapy based on the NGS findings. BCL2 p.A131V is classified as a somatic VUS
(score +5). Recommendation: inclusion in a clinical study or re-evaluation before targeted therapy.
Mandatory additional diagnostics before a decision: re-biopsy with immunophenotype (CD19/CD20), EBER,
CAR-T persistence qPCR, extended NGS including CNV/FISH and PET restaging. If actionability is
lacking: check allo-SCT or antigen-adapted CAR-T/ADC/tafasi-lenalidomide as part of
studies/guidelines.
Rationale:
Point analysis: OP4 +1, OP3 +1, OS2 +4, SBP1 -1 = Total +5 -> Classification: VUS. Despite OncoKB
being classified as likely oncogenic, the overall evidence remains insufficient (contrary in silico
results). Therefore no direct targeted recommendation; Study inclusion and renewed molecular/immune
characterization are recommended before treatment decisions are made.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Primary: inclusion in a venetoclax-containing clinical trial (e.g. ViPOR) or venetoclax-based salvage
therapy; If there is complete remission and patient suitability, consider Allo-SCT as consolidation.
Parallel alternatives: for CD19+/CD20+ Loncastux or tafasitamab+lenalidomide; If there is CD19 loss,
further CAR-T against another antigen (e.g. CD22) or participation in a study. PD-1 inhibitor for
EBV+ as an option in studies.
Rationale:
BCL2 p.A131V = Likely Oncogenic (+8; COSMIC-Hotspot, OncoKB). PubMed (PMID:38899693) supports
venetoclax-containing regimens (ViPOR) in R/R DLBCL. Mandatory before deciding on therapy: new biopsy
with immunophenotype (CD19/CD20), EBER, CAR-T persistence qPCR, NGS and PET restaging.

===== run_3 =====

If the Likely Oncogenic BCL2-p.A131V is present, a venetoclax-based combination salvage therapy (e.g.
ViPOR: venetoclax+ibrutinib+lenalidomide+obinutuzumab+prednisone) is recommended (evidence level II,
phase 1b/2). If there is a >=PR response, allogeneic stem cell transplantation should be considered
promptly; alternatively, participation in studies with BCL2 inhibitors.
Rationale:
BCL2 p.A131V is Likely Oncogenic (+8 points). PubMed article 1 (PMID38899693) demonstrates clinical
activity of venetoclax-based regimens in r/r DLBCL with this mutation. No relevant FISH
abnormalities. The patient is progressive after CAR-T and glofitamab, so targeted BCL2 inhibition is
a priority.
======================================================== gpt-oss-120b (plain)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:

an allogeneic stem cell transplant (allo-SCT) if the patient is transplant eligible. If you are not
suitable, you should consider taking part in a clinical study protocol (e.g. PD-1 blockade or
EBV-specific T-cell therapy).
Rationale:
The patient has an aggressive, EBV-positive DLBCL with multiple extranodal manifestations and has
already completed all established lines (R-CHOP variant, CAR-T, bispecific). Allo-SCT offers the only
potentially curative option after a multirefractory course; If transplantation is not possible,

===== run_2 =====

Rationale:

offer a useful supplement or study option.
===== run_3 =====

clinical trial with EBV-specific T cells or PD-1 blockade.

Rationale:
The patient is refractory to R-polatuzumab-CHP, CD19-CAR-T and CD20xCD3-bispecific. Further

evidence (PMID:38899693) supports the use of ViPOR (venetoclax, ibrutinib, prednisone, obinutuzumab,

Rationale:

tesirine is a standardized option for relapse/refractory DLBCL after >=2 prior therapies, according
to NCCN guidelines. It acts independently of CD20 expression and shows clinical efficacy even after
CAR-T failure.

======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Primary watch-and-wait: In the case of an isolated, histologically confirmed BRAF V600E-positive HZL
lesion in the humerus, without contrast infiltrate and with a normal blood count, we recommend
conservative observation with close monitoring. In case of pain, risk of fracture or radiological
progression: orthopedic evaluation +/- local radiotherapy. Systemic therapy (standard: cladribine +/-
rituximab) only in the case of cytopenia, systemic manifestation or after progression; BRAF inhibitor
(e.g. vemurafenib) reserved for contraindication to purine analogues or bridging.
Rationale:
Consistent with guidelines: Asymptomatic HZL without cytopenia does not fulfill an indication for
therapy -> W&W accepted. Bone lesions may be locally symptomatic/at risk of fracture, then local
therapy. The first-line standard for systemic disease is purine analogues (cladribine). BRAF
inhibitor off-label in AI against PA. Recommended staging/monitoring before/without therapy: CT
chest/abdomen/pelvis or PET-CT, sonography of the spleen, repeat contrast biopsy/pretherapeutic
immunophenotyping and confirmation BRAF, blood count every 3 months, imaging of the lesion
(X-Ray/MRI) every 2-3 months initially or if symptoms occur; Check vaccination status.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
We recommend: In the case of asymptomatic, isolated bone manifestation, normal blood count and no CM
involvement: Watch & Wait with close monitoring (blood count q4-12 weeks) and imaging control. In
case of pain/fracture risk or progression: local therapy (surgical stabilization if indicated +/-
radiotherapy). Systemic therapy only for hematological indication or systemic progression: First-line
purine analogue (cladribine s.c. 0.14 mg/kg x5) +/- rituximab.
Rationale:
Conforms to guidelines for asymptomatic classic HZL without cytopenias W&W; Bone lesions warrant
interventional/radiotherapeutic treatment if symptoms or risk of fracture are present. Systemic only
for cytopenias/progression: cladribine standard; BRAF inhibitor primarily when there is a
contraindication to purine analogues. Before therapy: complete staging (KM biopsy with
immunophenotype/BRAF, sonoabdomen, imaging MRI/PET-CT), basic lab, immune/vaccination status and
infection screening.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Observation (watch and wait) with close monitoring; no primary systemic therapy if CM and PB are
otherwise normal. If there is pain or risk of fracture: orthopedic evaluation +/- local radiotherapy
or stabilization. If systemic therapy is necessary later: Cladribine s.c. (+/- rituximab) first-line;
for AI against purine analogues: vemurafenib (+/- rituximab).
Rationale:
Contrast biopsy without infiltrate and normal blood count meet guideline criteria for W&W. Isolated
bone lesion is active disease with risk of fracture -> local therapy only if symptoms/instability.
Staging/monitoring before (and if necessary before start): complete blood count with diff,
liver/kidneys, abdominal US, imaging (MRI/CT or FDG-PET) of the lesion, infectious serology and
vaccination status. Controls: BB every 4 weeks initially, abdominal US q3M. Cladribine is standard
for systemic indications; BRAF inhibitor reserved for AI/bridging (for BRAF V600E dermatological
monitoring).

No systemic or local therapy indicated. Watch & Wait with blood count checks every 3 months and
imaging monitoring of the bone lesion (e.g. MRI/X-ray).
Rationale:
Isolated extramedullary manifestation with normal blood count, negative bone marrow and no symptoms
does not fulfill an indication for therapy (TI-1 flowchart). Systemic therapy is not indicated.
Consider local radiation only if the lesion is symptomatic (pain/risk of fracture). Watch & Wait with
regular monitoring is standard.

======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Primary recommendation: Start BTK inhibitor therapy, preferably zanubrutinib, as the next systemic
therapy. In case of intolerance or progression: if possible, inclusion in a study (non-covalent BTKi
or anti-CD38 study). Anti-CD20-based concepts are unsuitable because of complete CD20 loss;
autologous SCT is not primarily indicated.
Rationale:
CD20 negativity after rituximab makes further anti-CD20 strategies (e.g. R2) of little use.
Literature and congress data show BTKi (especially zanubrutinib) effective in R/R MZL after
anti-CD20. Plasma cell differentiation justifies testing of experimental anti-CD38 approaches or
clinical trials; Chemotherapy/SCT only for specific indications.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Primary initiation of a BTK inhibitor (e.g. zanubrutinib) as the next systemic therapy; priority for
inclusion in a study (non-covalent BTKi or BTKi combination). Do not re-administer Anti-CD20.
Anti-CD38/lenalidomide approaches or autologous SCT only in studies or selectively for specific
indications.
Rationale:
Complete loss of CD20 renders rituximab ineffective. For relapsed/refractory MZL after anti-CD20,
BTKi (zanubrutinib) is the most evidence-supported. New BTKi/studies offer additional options.
Anti-CD38, lenalidomide or auto-SCT have limited data and are preferably trial-based.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Further treatment: Start BTK inhibition with zanubrutinib (approved option for relapsed/refractory
MZL) as primary next systemic therapy. Parallel: Arrange pathology for CD38 IHC. If BTKi is
contraindicated/intolerable: systemic chemotherapy (e.g. bendamustine without anti-CD20) or inclusion
in a clinical study. Anti-CD38 (e.g. daratumumab) only in study/with strong CD38 positivity and
interdisciplinary consideration. ASCT is currently not recommended.
Rationale:
Complete CD20 loss renders anti-CD20 therapy ineffective. BTK inhibitors (zanubrutinib) are effective
and approved in MZL after anti-CD20. Plasmacellular differentiation warrants CD38 testing; The
anti-CD38 approach is experimental/off-label and only justified if there is evidence/study. ASCT is
not standard in indolent MZL.

======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
IIB with a mediastinal mass should be treated as advanced cHL. Recommendation: BrECADD (4-6 cycles)
with interim PET after 2 cycles. For DS1-3, a total of 4 cycles; for DS4-5 extension to 6 cycles.
Consolidative involved-site RT mediastinum 30 Gy only for PET-positive residual lesions. If BrECADD
is contraindicated: consider BEACOPesk. (fit, fertility education) or alternatively 4x ABVD + 30 Gy.
Rationale:
Mediastinal mass corresponds to GHSG-RF -> treatment as advanced stage. Guidelines/HD21: BrECADD <=60
y. offers high PFS and better tolerability vs BEACOPesque.; RT only if there is a PET-positive
residual. Recommend fertility advice/protection before alkylating therapy.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: Systemic first line with BrECADD, PET-adaptive. Interim PET after 2 cycles: DS1-3 ->
a total of 4 cycles; DS4-5 -> extend to 6 cycles or escalate according to protocol decision. Before
therapy: fertility advice/preservation. Consolidative involved-site RT of the mediastinum only if
residual PET findings are positive, goal: 30 Gy.
Rationale:
cHL Ann-Arbor IIB with a mediastinal mass is considered 'advanced' (bulk) according to the GHSG.
HD21/Onkopedia: BrECADD <=60 J. Standard due to high PFS and better toxicity vs BEACOPPesk. RT only
if there is a PET-positive residual (30 Gy IS-RT). Fertility protection due to alkylating
agents/BrECADD recommended.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
We recommend the PET-adapted GHSG scheme "2+2": 2 cycles BEACOPPesk -> interim PET. For DS 1-3: 2
cycles of ABVD, no IS-RT; for DS 4-5: Involved Site RT Mediastinum 30 Gy. If BEACOPP is not
acceptable due to age/comorbidity or fertility desire: 4x ABVD + IS-RT 30 Gy. Before starting
therapy: fertility advice, ECG/echo, lung function, PET/CT.
Rationale:
Stage IIB with mediastinal mass falls into the GHSG intermediate group. The PET-adapted 2+2 strategy
(HD-17) offers very good PFS and enables RT to be avoided in the case of PET negativity. BEACOPPesk
is more potent but significantly gonadotoxic; in ABVD, consolidative IS-RT (30 Gy) is recommended
with residual PET.
========================================================= gpt-5-mini (plain)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====

For classic HL, Ann Arbor IIB with mediastinal RF (RFc) and without previous treatment, we recommend
BrECADD therapy: 4-6 cycles (PET after 2 cycles, DS1-3 -> 4 cycles, DS4-5 -> 6 cycles). For
PET-positive residues, 30Gy RT (IS-RT).
Rationale:
StageIIBc corresponds to advanced HL (GHSGRS-2c). For patients <=60 years, BrECADD is the current
standard option (HD21 study) with high PFS and better tolerability than BEACOPPesk. PET-adapted cycle
adjustment and RT with positive residual are guideline-compliant.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
For classic HL, Ann Arbor IIB with mediastinal RF (RFc), we recommend the PET-adapted BrECADD regimen
as first-line therapy: 4 cycles (for DS1-3) or 6 cycles (for DS4-5) after 2 cycles. For PET-positive
residues, 30Gy IS-RT. Alternatively, BEACOPPesque. if there are contraindications to BrECADD.
Rationale:
Flowchart III-C-3 defines IIBc/d as advanced stages. Standard is 4-6xBrECADD + RT for PET-positive
residues. PET-adapted cycle optimization reduces stress. BEACOPPesque. remains as an alternative in
case of intolerance. There are no secondary diagnoses or contraindications.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
BrECADD 4-6 cycles (PET-adapted) + 30Gy RT for PET-positive residues.
Rationale:
StageIIB with RFc (mediastinal bulk) -> advanced classic HL. GHSG study HD21 defines BrECADD as the
standard for patients <=60 years old. PET control after 2 cycles controls the number of cycles (4 for
PET-neg, 6 for PET-pos). RT 30Gy with positive residual.

======================================================== gpt-oss-120b (agent)

===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
In classic HL, stage IIBE with mediastinal bulk (RFc), we recommend a PET-adaptable BrECADD regimen

======================================================== gpt-oss-120b (plain)

===== run_2 =====

===== run_3 =====

#### CASE: 14
