======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
After ISRT has been completed, we recommend: 1) First restaging primarily using EGD with multiple
staged biopsies 3 months after RT (alternatively 3-6 months). 2) No routine PET-CT and no routine
bone marrow biopsy. 3) Laboratory (BB, LDH, liver/kidney), HCV/HBV/HIV if necessary. 4) Follow-up:
EGD+Histo every 3-6 months in the first 2 years, then every 6-12-18 months; Laboratory checks every
3-6 months. 5) Systemic therapy only if progression is confirmed (endoscopic PD + Wotherspoon 5 /
symptomatic or disseminated disease). t(11;18) test if not carried out.
Rationale:
Gastric MALT responds late to RT (CR to 15 months); Guidelines recommend W&W and endoscopic control
every 3-6 months. PET/CT has low FDG avidity; Contrast biopsy not routine. Systemic therapy only in
the case of documented progression or symptomatic/systemic disease (R-bendamustine/rituximab etc.).
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
No immediate PET-CT or bone marrow biopsy. First restaging: EGD with multiple staged biopsies 3-4
months after completion of radiation, then EGD+histology every 3-6 months for 2 years, then interval
extension to 6-12 months (up to >5 years, if necessary annually). Laboratory controls (BB, LDH, beta2
microglobulin, liver/kidneys) at the same time. CT only if symptoms/clinical suspicion. Supplementary
systemic therapy only in cases of confirmed progression (endoscopically + histologically, e.g.
Wotherspoon 5/PD), symptomatic or disseminated disease; Preferred first-line therapies:
rituximab-bendamustine or rituximab monotherapy.
Rationale:
Response to ISRT can only be confidently assessed after 3-6 (up to 15) months; Avoid premature
escalation. PET/CT is not routinely helpful in gastric MALT due to low FDG avidity; Bone marrow
biopsy not required as standard. Guidelines/SOPs recommend endoscopic-histological checks at the
specified intervals and systemic therapy only in cases of documented progression or
symptomatic/spread disease.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
No immediate switch to systemic therapy. Restaging: EGD with staged biopsies 3-6 months after
completion of RT; Basic laboratory (BB, LDH, liver/kidney, HBV/HCV/HIV). PET-CT and bone marrow
biopsy not routine. Follow-up: EGD+biopsies every 3-6 months until CR (up to 12-24 months), then
every 6-12-18 months. Systemic therapy (e.g. rituximab+/-bendamustine) only in the case of
histologically/radiologically confirmed progression or symptomatic findings; Consider study
participation.
Rationale:
ISRT leads to high remission rates, CR may not occur for 3-6 (up to 15) months - avoid premature
escalation. PET has low sensitivity for MALT; Contrast biopsy is not routinely indicated. Systemic
therapy only if progression or symptoms are documented according to guidelines/flowchart.
========================================================= gpt-5-mini (plain)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Endoscopic restaging (EGD with multiple biopsies) 3 months after radiation. In case of complete
remission: close observation (EGD + biopsies every 6-12 months for 2-3 years, then annually up to 5
years). In the case of residual/progression: t(11;18) clarification and systemic therapy (rituximab
+/- chemotherapy, e.g. R-bendamustine). No further radiation.

Rationale:
In localized MALT (stage IEA), H.p. eradication + 30 Gy RT is curatively possible. First, response
documentation via EGD/biopsy. For CR, no further therapy; in the case of persistence/progression or
t(11;18)+, systemic therapy is indicated. RT has already been given, so do not repeat.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Observation after completed radiation therapy (30 Gy): EGD with biopsies in 3 months, then every 6
months for 2 years, then annually. CT if clinical symptoms or findings are suspected. No immediate
systemic therapy. In case of residual/progression: consider rituximab monotherapy or rituximab +
bendamustine.
Rationale:
In localized gastric MALT (stage IEA), 30 Gy radiation often leads to CR. H.p. has already been
eradicated and is negative. Therefore primary surveillance with endoscopic control; systemic therapy
only if persistence/progression is proven.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
EGD with targeted biopsies and H.p. test 3-6 months after radiation for restaging; In the case of
complete remission, conservative follow-up care (EGD every 6 months in the 1st year, then annually,
CT check if necessary). In the case of persistent/recurrent local or progressive findings, systemic
therapy with anti-CD20 (rituximab +/- chemotherapy, e.g. bendamustine-R) after interdisciplinary
consideration.
Rationale:
Stage IEA MALT of the stomach was treated locally with 30 Gy after H.p. eradication. Response to RT
may be delayed; The first goal is histological restaging. With CR, surveillance is sufficient. In the
case of residual/progression, systemic anti-CD20-based therapy is the standard option.

The combination of H.p. eradication and local radiation is the established standard for H.p.-negative
MALT lymphomas of the stomach. If there is a complete response and no progression, further therapy is
not indicated. Follow-up care serves to detect recurrences early.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: After eradication and radiotherapy (30 Gy), surveillance with EGD and CT H/T/Abd.
every 6-12 months. No further therapies required.
Rationale:
Stage IEA MALT lymphoma (H.p. negative) requires no further interventions after successful
radiotherapy. Surveillance is standard because the risk of recurrence is low and the response to
therapy is good. No progression or contraindications known.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Active surveillance with regular checks (endoscopy, CT) after radiation therapy has been completed.
No further therapies required.
Rationale:
H.p.-negative MALT lymphoma stage IEA showed complete regression after radiotherapy. No progression
or residual disease detected. Consensual surveillance is standard.

======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Primarily recommend CAR-T therapy if the patient is CAR-T capable. Before: re-biopsy and complete
restaging (PET/CT, laboratory, cardiac assessments) and rapid presentation to the CAR-T center. If
necessary, bridging therapy (Tumor board decision). If CAR-T is not possible: platinum-containing
salvage (e.g. R-DHAP/ICE) with interim PET and for CR/PR high-dose chemotherapy + auto-SCT.
Consolidative radiotherapy only in the case of residual mediastinal bulk/symptomatic local findings.
Rationale:
Early recurrence <=12 months after R-CHOP: Guidelines/flowchart prefer CAR-T if CAR-T is suitable
(better results than ASCT in this situation). Re-biopsy/restaging are mandatory. If CAR-T is not
feasible, classic platinum-containing salvage with the aim of HD-SZT is the established alternative;
Use radiation restrictively.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Priority clarification and rapid implementation of CAR T-cell therapy (2nd line), as recurrence
occurs within 12 months after R-CHOP. Before therapy: re-biopsy, complete restaging (PET/CT),
cardiological and infectious clarification (HBV/HCV/HIV). Bridging only individually; in case of
progression under R-RHAP, no repeating of the same platinum-containing regimen. Local radiation only
in the case of symptomatic mediastinal compression or as a palliative measure.
Rationale:
Early relapse <=12 months: randomized studies and current SOP/flowchart prefer CAR-T over platinum
salvage+autoSCT. Patient received 1 cycle of R-RHAP (salvage started) - if suitable for CAR-T,
primary CAR-T. High-dose HD/autoSCT only remains an alternative if CAR-T is not possible; RT only in
case of local urgency.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Re-biopsy and complete restaging; immediate check of CAR-T suitability. If CAR-T is suitable: primary
CAR-T therapy with suitable bridging (lymphoma Tumor board decision); In the case of symptomatic
mediastinal bulk, local radiation can be used as a bridging/cytoreductive measure. If not CAR-T
compatible: continue platinum-containing salvage (e.g. R-DHAP/R-ICE) and for CR/PR high-dose
chemotherapy + autologous stem cell transplant.
Rationale:
According to the SOP/flowchart, early recurrence (<=12 months after R-CHOP) is considered an
indication for primary CAR-T if CAR-T is suitable; Salvage + autoSCT is primarily used for late
recurrences (>12 months). Re-biopsy/restaging are mandatory for backup and therapy planning. A
one-time R-RHAP cycle is not sufficient as a reason for automatic continuation to autoSZT.

mm_case_1__ALL

======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Immediate induction with daratumumab s.c. + Bortezomib s.c. (weekly) + cyclophosphamide (reduced
dose, IV after dialysis) + dexamethasone (Dara-VCd). Lenalidomide should initially be avoided in
cases of renal insufficiency requiring dialysis. Clarify transplantation immediately; Stem cell
mobilization/apheresis after ~3 cycles with G-CSF +/- plerixafor; Minimize alkylator before
collection. thrombosis/infection prophylaxis; Check osteoprotection (if necessary denosumab if
dialysis).
Rationale:
For renal insufficiency requiring dialysis, Flowchart recommends Dara-VCd: initially avoids
lenalidomide (renal elimination), bortezomib does not require dose adjustment, daratumumab s.c.
better for COPD. Early ASCT clarification and prompt stem cell collection necessary; Give
cyclophosphamide post-dialysis and reduced.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Immediate induction with daratumumab subcutaneously + bortezomib s.c. (weekly) + cyclophosphamide +
dexamethasone (Dara-VCd). At the same time, rapid clarification of transplantability
(cardio/pulmonary/PS). After 3-4 cycles, early stem cell mobilization (G-CSF +/- Plerixafor) and ASCT
if suitable. Avoid lenalidomide initially during dialysis; Adjust maintenance after ASCT to renal
function.
Rationale:
Renal insufficiency requiring dialysis and multiple CRAB criteria require rapid treatment. Flowchart
recommends DaraVCd instead of lenalidomide-containing regimens for patients requiring dialysis.
Daratumumab s.c. preferred because of COPD and lower infusion reaction rate. Bortezomib and
cyclophosphamide effective in renal insufficiency; Bortezomib at usual dose (s.c., weekly),
cyclophosphamide, dose reduction if necessary depending on clinical status; Low dose dexamethasone.
Early mobilization/ASCT if fit; initiate osteoprotection, infection and thrombosis prophylaxis; If
kidney function improves, consider switching/maintenance with lenalidomide.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Begin immediate induction: daratumumab (preferably SC) + bortezomib (SC, weekly) + cyclophosphamide +
dexamethasone (Dara-VCd). In parallel: quickly clarify transplantability; If suitable, stem cell
mobilization after 3-4 cycles and planned ASCT. Avoid lenalidomide in dialysis or only with specific
medication. Dosing schedule.
Rationale:
Renal insufficiency requiring dialysis favors Dara-VCd (avoids lenalidomide problems). Daratumumab
s.c. favorable because of COPD. Rapid onset due to hypercalcemia, anemia and osteolysis. Bortezomib
s.c. good in renal failure. Plan mobilization early; HSV prophylaxis, thrombosis prophylaxis and
nephrology/transplant center coordination recommended.
======================================================== gpt-5-mini (plain)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Immediate induction treatment with a bortezomib-based triplet (VCD): bortezomib s.c. 1.3 mg/m2
d1,4,8,11 (cycle dependent) + cyclophosphamide (e.g. 300 mg/m2 p.o. d1,8,15) + dexamethasone (20-40
mg/weekly). If available and without a contraindication, supplementation with daratumumab is
recommended. Discontinue zoledronic acid and use denosumab 120 mg s.c. instead. q4w. Supportive:
rapid dialysis, correction of hypercalcemia, transfusion/ESA as needed, and infection prophylaxis.

Rationale:
In cases of renal insufficiency requiring dialysis, bortezomib-based regimens are the first choice
due to rapid improvement in kidney function and good effectiveness; Lenalidomide difficult to dose
during dialysis. Daratumumab increases remission rates. Zoledronic acid is nephrotoxic - Denosumab is
kidney independent.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Immediate cytoreductive induction with bortezomib-based triple therapy: VCd (subc. bortezomib +
cyclophosphamide + dexamethasone). If available and without contraindication, addition of daratumumab
is possible. Supportive: dialysis/acute hypercalcemia treatment, HSV prophylaxis, thrombosis
prophylaxis; Stop zoledronate and switch to denosumab. Check transplant suitability based on primary
response.
Rationale:
Renal insufficiency requiring dialysis and acute organ manifestation require rapid cytoreductive
therapy. Bortezomib is rapid-acting and safe in renal insufficiency; Cyclophosphamide initially
avoids lenalidomide problems during dialysis. Daratumumab can deepen remission. Bisphosphonates
(zoledronate) problematic in dialysis -> denosumab preferred.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Immediate induction with bortezomib-based triple (CyBorD): Bortezomib s.c. 1.3 mg/m2 D1,4,8,11 q21d;
Cyclophosphamide 300 mg/m2 p.o. weekly (adjust to dialysis timing); Dexamethasone 20-40 mg/week.
Acyclovir prophylaxis. discontinue bisphosphonate; Denosumab 120 mg s.c. q4w + Ca/VitD. Clarify stem
cell reserve and nephro-oncological coordination.
Rationale:
Urgent rapid cytoreduction due to anemia, hypercalcemia, osteolysis and renal insufficiency requiring
dialysis. Bortezomib works quickly and does not require dosage adjustment in kidney failure.
Zoledronate problematic in dialysis -> Denosumab preferred. Antiviral prophylaxis due to bortezomib;
ASCT evaluation after response.
========================================================= gpt-5-nano (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Induction MM: DaraVCd (daratumumab + bortezomib + cyclophosphamide + dexamethasone) as first-line
therapy. Denosumab instead of zoledronate in dialyzing NI. Then high-dose melphalan with autologous
SCT; If necessary, tandem SCT if high risk or no CR after first high dose.
Rationale:
Renal insufficiency, hypercalcemia, anemia and osteolysis speak against lenalidomide; Denosumab is
preferred for renal insufficiency. CD38-based induction with PI components is effective and well
tolerated. Early SCT improves PFS/OS in ISS III/R2-ISS III high risk; If CR is inadequate, consider
tandem SCT if necessary.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Induction: DaraVCd in MM requiring dialysis (ISS III/R2-ISS III). Replace zoledronate with denosumab.
Early SCT mobilization; Consolidation: HD-Melphalan 200 mg/m2; If necessary, second SCT if high risk.
Maintenance: Lenalidomide if kidney function is adequate; alternatively pomalidomide.
Rationale:
High-risk MM with renal insufficiency requires rapid, highly effective induction. DaraVCd is renal
compatible; Denosumab preferred in renal insufficiency. Early ASCT increases CR rate; Individualize
or adjust maintenance depending on kidney status.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Induction: DaraVCd as first-line therapy for MM requiring dialysis. Denosumab instead of zoledronate.
Maintenance: Lenalidomide if the course goes well (with dose adjustment). Early autologous stem cell
transplantation recommended; If necessary, second SZT/tandem per response.

Rationale:
High-risk MM (ISS III, R2-ISS III) with anemia, osteolysis, hypercalcemia, and renal insufficiency
requires aggressive induction and SCT consolidation. Denosumab makes sense in NI. HDT-ASCT improves
prognosis; Early transplantation makes sense; Depending on the course, possibly tandem according to
CR status.
======================================================== gpt-5-nano (plain)
========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Induction MM in patients requiring dialysis, ISS III: start bortezomib-based therapy (VD or VCD) with
dexamethasone; Aim for rapid remission and preparation for autologous SCT. Continue zolendronate;
Consider denosumab if there is a renal contraindication. Check SCT after response.
Rationale:
Bortezomib-based induction is renally safe and enables rapid cytoreduction in dialysis patients;

hypercalcemia legitimizes rapid remission.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Inductive treatment with bortezomib-based triplet (VCD or VD-Dexa) for 4-6 cycles. Bortezomib is
preferred for renal insufficiency requiring dialysis; Replace zoledronate with denosumab; address
hypercalcemia; then testing for autologous stem cell transplantation, if conditions apply.
Rationale:
Renal dialysis requires renal independence of therapy; Bortezomib-based regimens provide rapid
remission rates. Denosumab is more suitable than zoledronate for renal insufficiency. Goal: rapid
stress reduction and transplant option, if the general condition allows this.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Inductive MM therapy for IgGκ with renal insufficiency requiring dialysis: initiate bortezomib-based
triplet therapy (VCD) or alternatively Vd to achieve rapid cytoreduction. Continue depending on
response; Subsequently, if necessary, autologous SCT, if suitable. Continue zoledronate; Treat
hypercalcemia appropriately.
Rationale:
Bortezomib-based induction is preferred for renal damage (no renal dose adjustment required) and
provides rapid remission. High tumor burden (ISS III, R2) speaks in favor of triplet regimen. No
high-risk cytogenetics. SCT after response increases long-term control; Check reserves for
transplantation.
======================================================== gpt-oss-20b (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Start DaraVCd induction (Daratumumab 16mg/kg SC, Bortezomib 1.3mg/m2 SC qwk, Cyclophosphamide
300mg/m2 IV, Dexamethasone 40mg qwk) 4-6 cycles, then assess for autologous SCT if fit. Continue
bisphosphonate, infection and thrombosis prophylaxis.
Rationale:
Patient ISSIII, R2-ISSIII, dialyzed renal failure -> lenalidomide contraindicated. DaraVCd is
guideline-recommended for dialyzed patients, combining effective anti-CD38, PI and alkylator. SC
daratumumab mitigates asthma risk; weekly bortezomib limits neuropathy. Aggressive induction and SCT
are warranted for high-risk diseases.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Start daratumumab+bortezomib+cyclophosphamide+dexamethasone (DaraVCd) as an induction regimen.
Daratumumab (s.c. possible) offers high effectiveness in R2-ISSIII, bortezomib is dialysis-safe,
cyclophosphamide does not cause renal excretion. Lenalidomide not recommended due to renal

insufficiency. Supplement osteoprotection, infection and thromboprophylaxis.
Rationale:
Patient with ISSIII, R2-ISSIII, renal insufficiency requiring dialysis, anemia, osteolysis,
hypercalcemia. According to the flowchart, daratumumab + bortezomib + cyclophosphamide is the
recommended first-line therapy for renal insufficiency requiring dialysis. Lenalidomide would be
renal burden; Bortezomib subcutaneously reduces neuropathy. Daratumumab s.c. minimizes COPD infusion
reactions. Prophylaxis for bones, infection and thrombosis is mandatory.
===== run_3 =====

Start induction with DaraVCd (Daratumumab+Bortezomib+Cyclophosphamide+Dexamethasone) subcutaneously,
weekly, with renal-adjusted doses; evaluate for autologous stem-cell transplant if fit.
Rationale:
Dialysis-dependent renal insufficiency makes lenalidomide contraindicated. According to the
flowchart, DaraVCd is the recommended first line for dialysis patients. Subcutaneous daratumumab
administration reduces infusion reactions in COPD; Weekly bortezomib minimizes neuropathy,
cyclophosphamide is dialysis tolerant.

======================================================== gpt-oss-120b (agent)
========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:

cycles, then autologous stem cell transplantation (melphalan 200mg/m2, dose reduction if necessary)
if fitness is sufficient, followed by lenalidomide maintenance (dose-adjusted for dialysis).
Rationale:

guidelines, DaraVCd is the recommended first-line therapy for dialysis, combining CD-38 antibodies,

===== run_2 =====

clinical remission and sufficient fitness (e.g. after dialysis stabilization), autologous stem cell
transplantation with a reduced dose of melphalan. Maintenance with dose-adjusted
lenalidomide+bortezomib.

The patient meets SLiM-CRAB and has renal insufficiency requiring dialysis. According to the
guidelines, DaraVCd (or VRd) is recommended for this situation; Lenalidomide has a renal burden, so
CD38 antibody-based with cyclophosphamide is preferred. High-dose therapy only if you are
sufficiently fit, otherwise the regimen should be continued.
===== run_3 =====

then consolidation with autologous stem cell transplantation if renal insufficiency requiring
dialysis allows; otherwise received with dose-adjusted lenalidomide and osteoprotective therapy.
Rationale:
The patient meets SLiM-CRAB criteria, has high risk (ISSIII/R-ISSIII) and renal insufficiency
requiring dialysis. Bortezomib and cyclophosphamide are safe in renal insufficiency, while
daratumumab (subcutaneous) reduces respiratory risks. High-dose melphalan is problematic during
dialysis, therefore transplantation only if the kidneys are stable; otherwise received with
lenalidomide (dose-adjusted) and standard prophylaxis.
======================================================== gpt-oss-120b (plain)

response rate, melphalan is tolerated with dose reduction on dialysis. No known contraindications.
===== run_3 =====

cytogenetics, therefore standard triples recommended; Daratumumab may improve the depth of remission.
========================================================= qwen3-32b (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:

===== run_2 =====

===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommended therapy: DaraVCd (daratumumab s.c. + bortezomib + cyclophosphamide + dexamethasone) as
induction therapy. If the course goes well, maintenance with lenalidomide (dose adjustment if GFR <50
ml/min). Continue osteoprotection with zolendronate.
Rationale:
According to guidelines, DaraVCd is the preferred induction for renal failure requiring dialysis.
Cyclophosphamide replaces lenalidomide (renal excretion). Daratumumab s.c. reduces infusion risk in
COPD. Lenalidomide maintenance only if renal function is tolerable. Continue zolendronate for
osteoprotection.
======================================================== qwen3-32b (plain)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommended therapy initiation: VTD regimen (bortezomib, thalidomide, dexamethasone) with
dexamethasone dose adjustment due to COPD. Add denosumab instead of zoledronate due to the need for
dialysis.
Rationale:
VTD is preferred in high-risk myeloma with renal failure. Bortezomib is renal protective, thalidomide
tolerated in COPD, dexamethasone reduced to 20mg every 2 weeks. Denosumab arrests bone resorption
during dialysis.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:

duo therapy. Avoid lenalidomide due to renal insufficiency requiring dialysis.
Rationale:

<30 ml/min. ISS III/R2-ISS III requires effective therapy. Continue zoledronate for osteolysis
prophylaxis.

===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation for the initiation of therapy with bortezomib, lenalidomide and dexamethasone (VRd)
taking into account renal insufficiency.
Rationale:
VRd is standard induction therapy for newly diagnosed MM. Lenalidomide dose adjustment when requiring
dialysis, subcutaneous bortezomib tolerated. COPD and hypertension not relevant for choice of
therapy. Cytogenetics without high-risk factors. Hypercalcemia addressed by dexamethasone.

=========================================================
===== run_1 =====

======================================================= qwen3-next-80b (plain)
=======================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Start VRd regimen with lenalidomide dose adjustment (10 mg every 2 days), bortezomib 1.3 mg/m2 days
1,4,8,11, dexamethasone 40 mg weekly. Continue zolendronate.

===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Start D-Vd regimen (daratumumab, bortezomib, dexamethasone) as initial therapy.

Rationale:
D-Vd is optimal in renal insufficiency requiring dialysis because daratumumab and bortezomib are not
renally excreted. Lenalidomide requires dose adjustment and has fewer data in dialysis. D-Vd shows
high efficacy in ISS-III and is safe in COPD/hypertension.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Initiation of D-Vd therapy (daratumumab, bortezomib, dexamethasone) as first-line therapy
Rationale:
In cases of renal insufficiency requiring dialysis and ISS III/R2-ISS III, bortezomib-based therapy
is preferred. Daratumumab combined with bortezomib/dexamethasone (D-Vd) is evidence-based, non-renal
excretory, and safe in renal insufficiency. Lenalidomide requires dose adjustment, D-Vd offers better
risk stratification. COPD and hypertension are not contraindications.

======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)

===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
For high-risk MM (del17p, t(4;14), R2-ISS) and only PR after first autologous SCT: recommendation for
tandem autologous SCT (second high dose). Before second SCT, 1-2 cycles of consolidation with D-VRd
if tolerated; subsequently risk-adapted maintenance with lenalidomide + bortezomib. Before making a
decision, check MRD (NGS/flow >=10^-5) and PET/MRI and clarify stem cell reserve.
Rationale:
Rationale: High-risk cytogenetics and lack of CR after first HDT suggest second auto-SCT
(flowchart/guidelines). Consolidation can deepen remission before second SCT. MRD and imaging
objectify the need for therapy; Len+Boron as superior maintenance in high risk. Daratumumab in
maintenance only in studies/individual cases.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
In fit patients: carry out a second (tandem) autologous high-dose therapy/SCT. Subsequently,
intensive maintenance with lenalidomide + bortezomib (+/- CD38-AK only in studies). Carry out MRD
determination (NGS) and repeat FISH/extended molecular analyzes before the decision or promptly after
tandem.
Rationale:
Rationale: High-risk genetics (del17p, t(4;14)) and only PR after first ASCT justify more intensive
consolidation; Guidelines/flowchart recommend tandem for high risk. Combination PI+IMiD as
maintenance improves prognosis. MRD/repeat FISH for further risk stratification and therapy
adjustment.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: For high-risk cytogenetics (del17p, t(4;14)) and only PR after first high-dose
therapy -> preferred consolidation: second (tandem) autologous SCT now (stem cells present).
Thereafter maintenance: lenalidomide + concomitant bortezomib (+/- daratumumab in the study context).
Perform MRD (NGS/Flow) and advanced molecular panel before and after consolidation.
Rationale:
Rationale: High risk (R2-ISS VI) and lack of CR after first HDT justify intensification;

in study/in persistence.
======================================================== gpt-5-mini (plain)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: Consolidation with 2 cycles of the same regimen (DVrd/VRd) for deep induction after
ASCT. Subsequent high-risk maintenance: bortezomib (e.g. 1.3 mg/m2 s.c. q2w) plus lenalidomide (10-15

monotherapy. VZV and VTE prophylaxis; Close MRD/response monitoring.
Rationale:

primarily recommended.

===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:

with lenalidomide + bortezomib (PI+IMiD) until progression/intolerance. If tandem is not possible: 2
cycles of consolidation (D-)VRD before the above-mentioned maintenance. Consider clinical trials.
Rationale:
Patient only has PR after first auto-SCT and high-risk genes (del17p, t(4;14)). Data show that tandem
AutoSCT can improve response and PFS/OS in high-risk settings. Goal: Deep remission/MRD negativity.
In high-risk patients, proteasome inhibitor-assisted maintenance (bortezomib + lenalidomide) is more
effective than lenalidomide maintenance alone.
===== run_3 =====

(10-15 mg, 21/28) for >=2 years or until progression; close M-protein/FLC and MRD monitoring (q3-6M).

monotherapy is suboptimal in high-risk settings; Proteasome inhibitor-based maintenance (possibly
combined with IMiD) improves response/PFS. Consolidation can achieve deepening of remission. Consider
clinical trial/allo option in young, fit patient.

======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Change of drug class: immediate switch to a proteasome inhibitor-based regimen without CD38
antibodies and without lenalidomide (patient is progressive under DRD). For relevant peripheral
neuropathy, carfilzomib-based (e.g. KCd) is preferred; alternatively VCd or PVd if PNP is mild.
Parallel rapid stem cell mobilization and preparation for autologous SCT after response.
Rationale:
Progression under daratumumab+lenalidomide ⇒ CD38 and lenalidomide refractory. Patient is PI-naïve
(flowchart arm 2b): recommendation class switch to PI+IMiD/Alkylans and consolidation with autologous
SCT if suitable. Existing chemotherapy PNP reduces bortezomib suitability -> carfilzomib preferred;
no retreatment with CD38 antibodies.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Switch immediately to a proteasome inhibitor-based regimen, preferably carfilzomib-based (e.g. KCd:
carfilzomib + cyclophosphamide + dexamethasone) or carfilzomib + pomalidomide + dexamethasone; Avoid
bortezomib due to existing peripheral neuropathy. After tumor control, plan stem cell mobilization
and autologous SCT.
Rationale:
Progression under Dara+Len -> Patient is CD38 and lenalidomide refractory. According to the
algorithm, a PI-based regimen is indicated for CD38 pretreatment but PI-naïve patients. Pre-existing
chemotherapy-PNP speaks against bortezomib; Carfilzomib is a suitable alternative. ASCT occurs after
response; Venetoclax at t(11;14) only later/if necessary.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
If DRD progresses after ~4 cycles: Patient is considered lenalidomide and CD38 exposed/refractory.
Recommendation: immediate switch to a proteasome inhibitor-based, CD38-free regimen - preferably
carfilzomib-based (e.g. KCd or carfilzomib+pomalidomide+Dex). Avoid bortezomib due to existing PNP.
Parallel stem cell mobilization/collection and preparation for autologous SCT after response
(PR/>=VG). Venetoclax only check later/at t(11;14) in a later line.
Rationale:
DRD Progress = Class switch recommended. Patient is PI-naïve -> PI-based therapy is given priority.
Existing chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy militates against bortezomib; Carfilzomib is
therefore suitable. Goal: rapid disease control, strain collection and subsequent high-dose
therapy/auto-SCT. t(11;14) for later Venetoclax option.

======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Primary: inclusion in a clinical study - primarily MAPK-targeted studies (MEK inhibitor +/-
combination) or studies with non-BCMA immunotherapies (new CAR-T, bispecific against other targets).
If no study place: molecular confirmation of NRAS Q61R and, after informed consent, off-label MEK
inhibitor with close monitoring. Alternatively, Selinexor-Dex or trial Allo-SCT in suitable patients.
Rationale:
The patient has progressed after IMiD/PI/anti-CD38, 2xASCT, BCMA-CAR-T (ide-cel) and talquetamab -
renewed BCMA-directed therapy is probably less effective. NRAS Q61R activates the MAPK pathway and
provides a rational basis for MEK inhibition, although with limited prospective evidence; therefore
study priority, otherwise off-label only after confirmation and risk clarification.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: priority for inclusion in a clinical study (priority: non-BCMA-targeted
immunotherapies or studies on MAPK-targeted agents). If no study access: after MDT discussion and NGS
reconfirmation of NRAS Q61R off-label therapy with a MEK inhibitor (e.g. trametinib) including
complete baseline checks; alternatively palliative systemic therapy (e.g. Selinexor+dex).
Rationale:
Patient refractory to IMiD/PI/anti-CD38, 2x ASCT, BCMA-CAR-T (ide-cel) and GPRC5D bispecific ->
further BCMA-targeted options probably limited. NRAS Q61R indicates MAPK activation; MEK inhibition
is biologically plausible, but evidence is limited, hence a study priority. NGS reconfirmation and
MDT/monitoring required.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Primary: Inclusion in a clinical trial with NRAS-targeted therapy (e.g. MEK inhibitor +/-
combination) after renewed molecular confirmation. Alternatively: study-based non-BCMA immunotherapy
(other CAR/bispecific). Repeat BCMA-directed therapy only if there are no alternatives and after the
benefits and risks have been clarified.
Rationale:
Patient heavily pretreated (IMiD/PI/anti-CD38, 2x ASCT, BCMA-CAR, talquetamab) and NRAS Q61R
positive. Prior BCMA-CAR reduces the success of subsequent BCMA strategies. NRAS is rational goal
structure; MEK inhibitors have limited evidence, therefore study protection, molecular confirmation
as well as cardio/ophthalmological baseline and close monitoring are required.
======================================================== gpt-5-mini (plain)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Patient progressed after intensive previous therapy (2x HDM/ASCT, PI, IMiD, anti-CD38, CAR-T
(ide-cel), talquetamab). Primary: Inclusion in a clinical study with a new T cell/ADC approach
(preferably BCMA bispecific or alternative target GPRC5D/FcRH5 or alternative CAR-T). If no study:
Salvage with pomalidomide+cyclophosphamide+dexamethasone or selinexor-based.
Rationale:
After multiple treatment failures, an experimental therapy with a new target is the most promising.
Pomalidomide-based combination is rational standard option (pomalidomide not yet used). Selinexor is
an alternative palliative option for refractory MM. NRAS(Q61R) is currently not crucial for
treatment.

===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Priority: rapid forwarding and inclusion in a clinical study with novel active principles (e.g.
non-BCMA / non-GPRC5D bispecific antibodies or new CAR-T, CELMoDs). If study not available:
selinexor-based therapy (e.g. selinexor + pomalidomide + dexamethasone OR selinexor + bortezomib +
Dex). Palliative measures: Radiation therapy for painful osteolysis, bisphosphonates/denosumab.
Allo-SCT only in young, fit patients and with a suitable indication.
Rationale:
Patient is highly relapsed and refractory to IMiD, PI, anti-CD38, BCMA-CAR-T and GPRC5D bispecific
(talquetamab) - standard options exhausted. Clinical studies with new targets/mechanisms offer the
best chance. Selinexor combinations have proven activity in penta/relapsed cases. NRAS Q61R is
currently not directly targetable.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Patient with multi-refractory MM (PIs, IMiDs, anti-CD38; BCMA-CAR-T failed; talquetamab failed):
priority inclusion in clinical study with new mechanism of action (e.g. CELMoD, new bispecific/ADC or
MAPK-directed studies because of NRAS Q61R). Until trial: bridging with selinexor + dexamethasone
(+/- pomalidomide) or palliative cycles as tolerated.
Rationale:
Multiple refractory and progression after BCMA-CAR-T and GPRC5D bispecific antibody -> best chance
with experimental substances. NRAS Q61R warrants review of MAPK/MEK studies. Selinexor-based regimen
is a tested option for bridging in late lines of therapy.

is the best available therapy. Studies show response even after CAR-T progression. No
contraindications (no allergies, NRAS mutation does not affect effectiveness). Alternatives such as
Selinexor are less effective in this line.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Initiation of selinexor (80 mg weekly) in combination with dexamethasone (20 mg weekly) as the next
line of therapy
Rationale:
Patient has undergone all common therapies (PI, IMiD, anti-CD38, CAR-T, GPRC5D bispecifics).
Selinexor is approved for multiple relapsed MM after >=4 lines. NRAS mutation does not affect the
election. No known contraindications. Combination offers effective option with limited remaining

therapies.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Initiation of selinexor 80 mg once weekly plus dexamethasone 20 mg weekly
Rationale:
Patient has progression on multiple lines of therapy (BCMA-CAR-T, talquetamab, PI, IMiD, anti-CD38).
Selinexor is approved for refractory myeloma after >=4 lines of therapy and offers a new mechanism of

Rationale:
Patient progressing on lenalidomide (Len-refractory). Previous exposure to daratumumab with atrial
flutter as well as pre-existing tachyarrhythmic events and PVI increase the risk of
anti-CD38/carfilzomib-associated complications. PVd is effective in Len-refractory cases and is more
cardiac compatible; Apixaban covers VTE risk profile.

======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
The patient is generally a candidate for BCMA-directed therapy (CAR-T or BCMA-Bispecific/ADC) if
clinically and logistically suitable. BRAF p.N581I (Likely Oncogenic) allows consideration of
BRAF/MEK inhibition preferably in clinical trials or as short-term bridging; Allo-SCT only in young,
fit patients and there are no alternatives or after interdisciplinary consideration.
Rationale:
BRAF p.N581I was prepared according to Horak et al. classified as Likely Oncogenic (9 points).
Evidence: Phase 2 data for encorafenib+binimetinib in BRAFV600E-MM (PMID:36608320); direct data for
N581I are missing. BRAF mutation does not contraindicate BCMA therapy. Recommendation: BCMA therapy
primary; BRAF/MEK inhibitors in study/compassionate use as bridging; Check Allo-SCT if suitable.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Patient is still a candidate for BCMA-directed therapy (priority: BCMA-CAR-T or BCMA-bispecific/ADC).
Short-term bridging to CAR-T: dexamethasone-based regimen +/- cyclophosphamide or
pomalidomide/carfilzomib-based salvage therapy or daratumumab re-challenge depending on previous
therapies. Only consider allo-SCT in chemosensitive disease and fit patients. BRAF-targeted therapy
only as part of a study or after additional confirmation of function (not primary option).
Rationale:
BRAF p.N581I was prepared according to Horak et al. classified as Likely Oncogenic (+6) (functional
evidence, OncoKB). Publication shows benefit of BRAF/MEK in BRAFV600E (Phase II), evidence for N581I
limited. BCMA therapies are independent of BRAF status and are priority. BRAF/MEK only with admission
to study or clear functional confirmation.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
BRAF p.N581I (Likely Oncogenic). Primary: Patient remains a candidate for BCMA-directed therapy
(CAR-T or bispecific/ADC) and this should be pursued as a priority. In the case of rapid
progression/bridging: testing a BRAF/MEK therapy (e.g. encorafenib+binimetinib) preferably as part of
a study or off-label based on risk of benefit; Allo-SCT only if responsive/suitable performance.
Rationale:
BRAF p.N581I is according to Horak et al. classified as Likely Oncogenic (+6, OncoKB functional
evidence). Phase 2 data supports BRAF/MEK in BRAFV600E-MM; direct data for N581I are limited, but
functional evidence supports targetability. The mutation does not rule out BCMA therapies.
Recommendation: study inclusion and orthogonal confirmation of the mutation.

======================================================== gpt-oss-20b (agent)

===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:

binimetinib 45mg BID (Phase 2 data for BRAF-mutant MM, see PMID36608320). Alternatively, if

abnormalities, so there are no other therapy-based stomach conditions. PubMed evidence (PMID36608320)
reports a phase 2 trial (encorafenib + binimetinib) with antitumor activity in MM patients with BRAF
mutations. Because the mutation is activated, BRAF/MEK inhibition is the only targeted option after

Variant BRAFp.N581I receives 12 points (Likely Oncogenic) according to Horak-SOP and is oncogenic in
OncoKB. Phase II study (Giesen etal., 2023) demonstrates effectiveness of BRAF/MEK blockade in MM
patients with BRAFV600E; There is evidence-based evidence that other BRAF mutations (e.g. N581I) may
also respond to this therapy. Therefore, the combination is recommended as preferred, accelerated
therapy.

======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Priority: BCMA-directed therapy - preferred CAR-T (cilta-/ide-cel) if available. If not: BCMA
bisspecifics (e.g. teclistamab) or BCMA ADC. Addition: timely inclusion of studies in
RAS/MAPK-directed studies (MEK/ERK) is recommended. Allo-SCT only if there is no CAR-T option, good
fitness and preferably as part of a study.
Rationale:
NRAS p.Q61R classified as Likely Oncogenic (Horak et al.). Literature (PMID:24335104,27458004) shows
high prevalence in refractory MM and reduced sensitivity to PI (bortezomib). Therefore, avoid PI
monotherapy; prioritize BCMA-targeted therapies (talquetamab was GPRC5D) and RAS/MAPK inhibitor
trials; Allo-SCT only restrictive.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Priority: BCMA-directed therapy. Preferred BCMA CAR-T (e.g. cilta-cel/ide-cel) if available;
alternatively BCMA bisspecifics (e.g. teclistamab) or BCMA ADC. Parallel/subsequent inclusion in
clinical studies with RAS/MAPK inhibitors is recommended. Allo-SCT only if there is no CAR-T/study
access and good transplant tolerance.
Rationale:
NRAS p.Q61R = Oncogenic (Horak +10). Literature (PMID:24335104, PMID:27458004) shows high prevalence
of NRAS in refractory MM and reduced response to bortezomib alone -> avoid single-PI. NRAS justifies
the review of RAS/MAPK-directed studies, but does not represent a contraindication for BCMA
therapies.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
NRAS p.Q61R is classified as an oncogenic. Primary recommendation: BCMA-directed therapy with
priority - CAR-T (e.g. cilta-cel/ide-cel) if suitable; if not available/no CAR-T: BCMA-bispecifics
(e.g. teclistamab) or participation in BCMA-directed studies/ADC. Avoid further proteasome inhibitor
monotherapies. Allo-SCT only with selection criteria/study options or after CAR-T failure.
Rationale:
Classification: NRAS p.Q61R = Oncogenic (+10 points). Literature (PMID:24335104, 27458004) shows NRAS
Q61 mutations are common in refractory MM and associated with reduced PI sensitivity; supports
prioritization of BCMA immunotherapies and recruitment into MAPK-targeted trials. Use allo-SCT
cautiously due to the risk of mortality.

Rationale:
NRASp.Q61R scores 10 points (Oncogenic). Literature (PMID24335104,27458004) shows that NRAS mutations
induce bortezomib resistance and significantly increase sensitivity to PI/MEK inhibitors. Evidence:
retrospective MM cohorts, phase II data. Therefore, officially suitable MEK therapy.

===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Based on the oncogenic assessment of NRAS p.Q61R (10 points), therapy should be aimed at MAPK
signaling pathway inhibition. Immediate inclusion in a study with a MEK inhibitor (e.g. selumetinib
or trametinib) combined with a proteasome or IMiD component (e.g. pomalidomide) is recommended.
Additionally, BCMA-CAR-T therapy should be examined as a standard option for refractory PCL.

(PMID:24335104, 27458004) show reduced bortezomib efficacy and benefit of MEK/ERK inhibitors in

remains an important, non-variant-specific therapy option.

(pomalidomide) and dexamethasone (Phase II/III, evidence level B). Alternatively, bortezomib should
be avoided due to proven resistance and an alternative proteasome inhibitor (e.g. carfilzomib) should
be used.
Rationale:
The NRAS Q61R mutation is oncogenic (hotspot, functional evidence, missing population). PubMed
studies (PMID:24335104, PMID:27458004) show reduced bortezomib sensitivity and association with
refractory myeloma; MAPK inhibition is discussed as an effective strategy. No treatment-relevant FISH
results available.

an off-label MEK inhibitor can be considered.
Rationale:
NRASp.Q61R is described according to Horak et al. Classified as Oncogenic (+10) in 2022. Literature
(PMID:24335104, PMID:27458004) shows reduced bortezomib sensitivity and recommends Ras/MEK inhibition
in refractory MM. No other FISH changes available.

===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
We recommend transitioning to BCMA-directed therapy. A BCMA-CAR-T product (ide-cel or cilta-cel) is
preferred if the approval criteria are met; alternatively, belantamab-mafodotin can be used.
Rationale:
The patient is triple class refractory (PI, IMiD, anti-CD38) and progressive after GPRC5D bispecific
(talquetamab). No high-risk genetics, good performance and no contraindications for cell therapy.
BCMA targeting delivers the highest efficacy at this advanced stage.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Further BCMA-directed therapy: Implementation of BCMA CAR T cell therapy (e.g. Ide-cel) after
appropriate lymphocyte depletion; if not suitable, use belantamab-mafodotin.
Rationale:
Patient has progressive myeloma disease after >=7 lines of therapy (IMiD, PI, CD38 mAb, GPRC5D
bispecific), no high-risk genetics, remains transplant eligible. BCMA targeting provides the highest
expected response rate in this refractory setting.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: Implement BCMA-targeted CAR-T therapy (e.g. Ide-celt) as the next systemic treatment.
Alternatively: BCMA bisspecific (teclistamab) or selinexor-pomalidomide combination if CAR-T is not
possible.
Rationale:
Patient has refractory MM after IMiD, PI, anti-CD38 and GPRC5D bispecific, without high-risk
genetics. CAR-T offers the highest deep remission in multi-refractory disease; other options only if
contraindicated.
========================================================= qwen3-32b (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation for study inclusion in RAS-targeted therapies and evaluation of MEK inhibitors.
Avoiding bortezomib/IMiD monotherapies due to NRAS-p.Q61R-associated resistance.
Rationale:

The NRAS p.Q61R variant is classified as oncogenic (10 points) and is associated with resistance to
bortezomib (PMID:24335104) and IMiDs/proteasome inhibitors (PMID:27458004). Targeted therapies for
RAS mutations in myeloma are in clinical trials (e.g. MEK inhibitors), standard options are not
evidence-based.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation of a MEK inhibitor-based combination therapy (e.g. trametinib) in a clinical trial due
to the NRAS p.Q61R mutation and resistance to PIs/IMiDs
Rationale:
The NRAS p.Q61R mutation (Oncogenic, 10 points) correlates with resistance to bortezomib and IMiDs
(PMID:24335104, 27458004). Direct RAS targeting therapies are not established; MEK inhibitors as a
downstream target in the RAS pathway are being evaluated in clinical studies. Study inclusion for
combination with new RAS modulators is recommended.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: Therapy with MEK inhibitors (e.g. trametinib) or inclusion in clinical studies on RAS
pathway targeting. Avoid bortezomib monotherapy due to NRAS-p.Q61R-associated resistance.
Rationale:
The NRAS p.Q61R variant is classified as oncogenic (10 points). PubMed evidence shows resistance to
bortezomib and standard therapies. MEK inhibitors have preclinical activity in RAS-mutant tumors.
Study inclusion should be urgently considered for refractory myeloma.

======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: If t(11;14) (FISH 36%) is confirmed, give priority to a venetoclax-based regimen
(e.g. venetoclax + dexamethasone +/- proteasome inhibitor) because of t(11;14)-selective
effectiveness (phase II/subgroup findings). In parallel or as a consequence: BCMA-directed therapy
(CAR-T, e.g. cilta-cel, or BCMA bisspecifics/ADC) preferably in a clinical trial. Consider allo-SCT
only in young/fit patients after remission. Before deciding on therapy: repeat FISH/NGS, BCL2 test
and trial screening.
Rationale:
Rationale: t(11;14) (36%) is predictive of BCL2 dependence and response to venetoclax (Phase II and
subgroup analyses). BCMA-CAR-T and bispecifics show high remission rates in refractory MM; suitable
if there is no BCMA resistance yet. 1q-gain and del13q have a negative prognosis -> trial/highly
effective therapy recommended.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: Prioritize BCMA-targeted cell therapy (ciltacabtagene autoleucel / idecabtagene
vicleucel) if possible based on the patient. If rapid cytoreduction or CAR-T is not available: BCMA
bispecific (e.g. teclistamab) or venetoclax-containing combination (due to t(11;14), off-label) as
bridging. Prefer clinical studies. Allo-SCT only in young/fit people after CR and in studies;
Consider high risk.
Rationale:
Rationale: FISH shows t(11;14) (36%) - predictive of BCL2 dependency; Venetoclax is effective in
phase II/subgroup analyses. Patient is BCMA-naïve -> approved BCMA-CAR-T and BCMA-Bispecials show
high remission rates in R/R MM (Phase II/III). Plasma cell leukemia is high risk; Allo-SCT should
only be tested selectively/clinically.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: 1) Primarily a BCMA-directed therapy (if available CAR-T e.g. cilta-cel/ide-cel or
BCMA-bispecific such as teclistamab) as the next line (approval/phase II-III). 2)
Alternative/parallel venetoclax-based therapy for t(11;14) (preferably in a study or off-label with
monitoring). In cases of deep remission, consider allo-SCT as consolidation. Prefer clinical studies.
Rationale:
No somatic oncogene variants. FISH: t(11;14) (36%) - predictive of venetoclax responsiveness (Phase
II/subgroup analyses). Gain 1q21 -> unfavorable. BCMA-CAR-T and BCMA-bispecifics show high ORR in
refractory MM (Phase II/III, approvals). Allo-SCT only if remission has been achieved and suitable
fitness; Studies preferred.

combination with pomalidomide/dexamethasone in the phase 2 study and is suitable for patients with
prior BCMA therapy. If BOMP does not work, CAR T cells are the current standard option for
multi-refractory MM. Secondary diagnoses (allergies, appendectomy) are not contraindications.

recommended by guidelines.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommend BCMA-targeted CAR T-cell therapy (e.g. ciltacabtagene autoleucel).
Rationale:
Patient has undergone multiple lines of therapy including CD38 and GPRC5D targeted therapies with no
response. BCMA-targeted CAR-T therapies demonstrate high response rates in advanced RRMM in the
absence of prior BCMA therapy. No relevant contraindications (hay fever/appendectomy irrelevant).
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: CAR T-cell therapy with ciltacabtagene autoleucel (Carvykti).
Rationale:

===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:

======================================================== gpt-5-mini (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: Induction with daratumumab + bortezomib + lenalidomide + dexamethasone (Dara-VRd) for
3 cycles (Dara s.c. preferred). Stem cell mobilization after cycle 3 with G-CSF +/- cyclophosphamide
and collection of sufficient CD34+ cells. Consolidation: high-dose therapy with autologous SCT
(melphalan 200 mg/m2, 1x). Lenalidomide maintenance (10 mg). Continue therapeutic anticoagulation;
Lenalidomide initially reduced dose; Initiate osteoprotection and infection prophylaxis.
Rationale:
Rationale: SLiM-CRAB (disseminated osteolysis) makes active therapy mandatory. For R-ISS II and no
contraindications, the HDT concentric approach is standard; According to the guidelines, Dara-VRd is
considered to be the strongest induction. Early mobilization after 3 cycles avoids mobilization
problems caused by prolonged exposure to lenalidomide. Z.n. LAE requires continued therapeutic
anticoagulation and careful lenalidomide dose initiation; no high-risk genetics -> no tandem SCT.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Recommendation: Induction with Dara-VRd (daratumumab s.c. + bortezomib + lenalidomide +
dexamethasone) for 3-4 cycles; Stem cell mobilization after cycle 3 (G-CSF +/- Plerixafor); then
high-dose therapy with autologous SCT (melphalan 200 mg/m2) as consolidation; then lenalidomide
maintenance. Initiate osteoprotection and prophylaxis.
Rationale:
SLiM-CRAB (disseminated osteolysis) and R-ISS II justify initial therapy. Dara-VRd achieves deep
remissions before ASCT (guideline). Mobilization after cycle 3 minimizes collection problems caused
by lenalidomide. If there is a history of VTE, continue therapeutic anticoagulation and initially
reduce the lenalidomide dose; Thrombosis prophylaxis, infection and bone protection are mandatory.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Patient with symptomatic MM (R-ISS II, standard risk). Recommendation: Induction with Dara-VRd
(daratumumab + bortezomib + lenalidomide + dexamethasone) for 3-4 cycles; Stem cell mobilization
after cycle 3-4 (G-CSF +/- cyclophosphamide; plerixafor if necessary); subsequent autologous
high-dose therapy (melphalan 200 mg/m2) as consolidation; Maintenance Lenalidomide 10 mg. If Dara-VRd
not available/off-label: VRd.
Rationale:
SLiM-CRAB (disseminated osteolysis) requires immediate systemic therapy. Dara-VRd increases response
rate before ASCT; Standard risk and R-ISS II justify a single ASCT. Continue therapeutic
anticoagulation due to previous pulmonary embolism; Reduce lenalidomide initially (1st cycle) and
thrombosis prophylaxis/coordination with hemostaseology. Additionally: osteoprotection
(bisphosphonate/denosumab) and infection prophylaxis.
======================================================== gpt-5-mini (plain)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
Beginning of systemic induction. If suitable for ASCT: 4 cycles of D-VRd (daratumumab + bortezomib +
lenalidomide + dexamethasone) -> stem cell mobilization and high-dose therapy/ASCT. If you are not
suitable for ASCT: D-Rd. Continue therapeutic anticoagulation and, with lenalidomide, thrombosis
prophylaxis; Bisphosphonates, pain and infection prophylaxis.
Rationale:

Symptomatic MM with disseminated osteolysis, R-ISS II, standard risk and no previous systemic
therapy. Daratumumab-based induction achieves high remission rates before ASCT; If there is a known
pulmonary embolism, anticoagulation/thrombosis prophylaxis is mandatory. Supportive measures
(bisphosphonates, VZV prophylaxis) are recommended.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Initiation of systemic first-line therapy. If transplantable: induction with
daratumumab-bortezomib-lenalidomide-dexamethasone (Dara-VRd) 3-4 cycles, stem cell
mobilization/harvest and subsequent autologous stem cell transplantation. If not transplantable:
Dara-Rd. Always therapeutic anticoagulation/strict thrombosis prophylaxis because of previously known
pulmonary embolism, zoledronic acid q4w for bone protection and antiviral prophylaxis (e.g.
acyclovir).
Rationale:
standard risk MM without previous systemic therapy; Dara-VRd achieves high remission rates and is
first-line for transplant recipients. History PE requires therapeutic anticoagulation rather than
just aspirin. Osteolysis warrants bisphosphonate. Infection prophylaxis recommended with CD38
antibodies/proteasome inhibitors.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Start of first-line systemic therapy with daratumumab + bortezomib + lenalidomide + dexamethasone
(Dara-VRd) as induction; Parallel clarification of autologous transplant suitability. If transplant
eligible, alternative: daratumumab + lenalidomide + dexamethasone (Dara-Rd). Continue therapeutic
anticoagulation for PE and initiate HSV prophylaxis.
Rationale:
Symptomatic MM with disseminated osteolysis and no previous systemic therapy. Dara-based induction
achieves high remission rates and allows stem cell mobilization with standard risk cytogenetics.
History of pulmonary embolism requires therapeutic anticoagulation and adjustment of VTE prophylaxis
with lenalidomide; Acyclovir because of bortezomib.
========================================================= gpt-5-nano (agent)
=========================================================
===== run_1 =====
Tumor board decision:
first-line induction with DaraVRd or IsaVRd; then consolidation with autologous SCT; subsequent
maintenance with lenalidomide; local therapies only for acute indications; consistent
thromboembolism, infection and osteoprotection.
Rationale:
SLiM-CRAB fulfilled; R-ISS II, standard risk karyotype; ED-MM despite local treatment; expected to be
fit for high-dose SCT; SCT improves depth of remission; CD38-naïve; no previous therapies;
Prophylaxis (VTE, infections, osteoprotection) is essential.
===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Induction: DaraVRd or IsaVRd; Consolidation: Autologous stem cell transplantation; Maintenance:
Lenalidomide 10 mg. Continue thromboembolism prophylaxis for LAE; Reduce lenalidomide dose in Cycle
1. Local RT only for acute symptoms/neurological deficits; further local therapies depending on
symptoms.
Rationale:
SLiM-CRAB positive, active MM despite standard risk; potentially SCT-eligible patient; Triplet
induction increases response rate, SCT enables deep remission; LAE requires anticoagulation; Only use
local therapies symptomatically.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Induction: DaraVRd (daratumumab + bortezomib + lenalidomide + dexamethasone) as first-line in
SLiM-CRAB+, R-ISS II. Check suitability for high-dose therapy; If suitable, autologous SCT (melphalan
200 mg/m2) for consolidation, followed by lenalidomide maintenance. No additional local therapy
necessary. Prophylaxis: osteoprotection, infection and thromboembolism; Reduce lenalidomide dose in

cycle 1 if history of VTE.
Rationale:
SLiM-CRAB+, R-ISS II, standard risk; no high-risk genetics. Systemic first-line makes sense after
spinal RT; SCT increases CR rate and long-term survival. Local therapies not necessary. History of
VTE requires anticoagulation and reduction of lenalidomide dose in cycle 1. Maintenance advisable.
======================================================== gpt-5-nano (plain)
========================================================
===== run_1 =====

Induction treatment for newly diagnosed ED MM standard risk: VRd induction
(bortezomib-lenalidomide-dexamethasone) 3-4 cycles, followed by autologous SCT if transplantable;
then lenalidomide maintenance. VTE prophylaxis due to previous PE (DOAC or LMWH).

===== run_2 =====
Tumor board decision:
Induction EDMM: daratumumab + bortezomib + lenalidomide + dexamethasone (D-VRd) for 4 cycles; ASCT if
eligible; then consolidation (Rd) and maintenance lenalidomide; Thromboprophylaxis with apixaban 2.5
mg BID; local RT only for symptomatic lesions.
Rationale:

outcomes in transplant-eligible patients; Thrombosis prophylaxis is essential due to a history of
VTE; RT only if symptomatic.
===== run_3 =====
Tumor board decision:
Induction for extramedullary MM from solitary plasmacytoma: Initiate
daratumumab-bortezomib-lenalidomide-dexamethasone induction (D-VRd) for 4-6 cycles. If
transplantable: ASCT followed by consolidation and long-term maintenance (lenalidomide +/-
daratumumab). For symptomatic lesions, add radiotherapy.
Rationale:
ED-MM requires robust CD38-targeted PI-based induction; D-VRd shows good effectiveness. If suitable,
ASCT makes sense; Standard risk, no previous treatments. Radiotherapy only for local symptoms.

===== run_2 =====

Autologous SCT improves survival, maintenance with lenalidomide prolongs remission.
===== run_3 =====

stem cell transplantation (melphalan200mg/m2) and then lenalidomide maintenance. In case of
comorbidity or frailty: DaraRd or VRd instead of DaraVRd.

to guidelines. DaraVRd offers the highest remission rate, autologous SCT improves survival,
lenalidomide maintenance extends progression-free time. Comorbidities would require alternative
regimens.

ASCT improves OS, maintenance extends PFS.
===== run_3 =====

cell transplantation. Schedule prophylactic anticoagulation for prior pulmonary artery embolism.
Rationale:
Patient has not yet received systemic therapy, R-ISSII, standard risk cytogenetics and no
contraindications to proteasome inhibitors. Lenalidomide increases risk of thrombosis; therefore
anticoagulation is necessary. VRd is the evidence-based first-line option for transplant-eligible or
